meta.title: Go
meta.description: Review checklist for Go services and libraries

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain what the change does, why it is needed, and any context a reviewer needs to understand the diff.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a clear description, reviewers waste time reverse-engineering intent from code. A good description speeds up review, reduces misunderstandings, and creates a searchable record for future developers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand the purpose and motivation without reading the diff? Is there a link to an issue or design doc?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you expand the PR description? It would help to understand why the handler middleware was restructured and what problem it solves.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves more reviewer time than it costs to write.

items.text: Is the change scoped appropriately, or should it be split into smaller PRs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should address one concern. Mixing a bug fix with a refactor with a new feature makes review harder and rollbacks riskier.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large, multi-concern PRs are slow to review, prone to rubber-stamping, and difficult to revert if one part causes issues. Smaller PRs get faster, higher-quality reviews.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently? Does the diff include both feature work and unrelated cleanup?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds the new gRPC endpoint and also refactors the database layer. Could we split these into two PRs so each can be reviewed and rolled back independently?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per PR. If you need a paragraph to explain what it does, it is probably too big.

items.text: Are there any unrelated changes bundled in?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Drive-by fixes, formatting changes, or unrelated tweaks included alongside the main change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unrelated changes obscure the actual diff, make git blame less useful, and complicate reverts. They also make the reviewer's job harder by mixing signal with noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Scan the file list. Are there files that do not seem related to the PR's stated purpose? Are there formatting-only changes in files otherwise untouched?
baseContent.exampleComment: The import reordering in handler.go looks unrelated to the caching changes. Mind pulling those into a separate PR so this one stays focused?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep PRs focused. Save drive-by fixes for a dedicated cleanup PR.

items.text: Is dead code removed rather than commented out?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Code that is no longer used should be deleted, not commented out. Version control preserves history if you ever need it back.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Commented-out code creates confusion about whether it is temporarily disabled or a leftover. It adds noise and often never gets cleaned up.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for large blocks of commented-out code. Check for unused functions or variables that the compiler or linter flags.
baseContent.exampleComment: This commented-out handler block has been here since the original implementation. Since we have git history, let us remove it to keep things clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Delete dead code. Git remembers so you do not have to.

items.text: Are TODO/FIXME comments accompanied by a tracking ticket?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TODO and FIXME comments should reference a ticket number so the work is actually tracked and not forgotten.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untracked TODOs accumulate as tech debt that nobody owns. With a ticket, the work enters the backlog and can be prioritized.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for TODO, FIXME, HACK, or XXX comments. Do they include a ticket reference?
baseContent.exampleComment: This TODO does not have a tracking ticket. Could you file one and reference it here? Otherwise it will likely get forgotten.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// TODO: handle pagination
users, err := repo.FetchAll(ctx)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// TODO(PROJ-1234): add pagination support for large result sets
users, err := repo.FetchAll(ctx)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every TODO should have an owner. A ticket number is the simplest way to assign one.

items.text: Does the commit history tell a coherent story?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commits should be logically organized with clear messages. Each commit should represent a meaningful, atomic unit of work.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good commit history helps future developers understand why changes were made, simplifies bisecting bugs, and makes reverts surgical instead of all-or-nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read through the commit log. Are messages descriptive? Are there WIP or fixup commits that should have been squashed? Does the sequence make logical sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: The commit history has several 'wip' and 'fix' commits. Could you squash these into logical units before merge? It will make the history much easier to navigate.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write commits for the person who will read git log in six months.

items.text: Are package names short, lowercase, singular nouns (not `utils`, `common`, `helpers`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go package names should be short, lowercase, single-word nouns that describe what the package provides. Names like utils, common, or helpers are discouraged because they say nothing about the package's purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Package names are part of every call site (e.g., http.Get, json.Marshal). Short, descriptive names lead to readable code. Vague names like utils become dumping grounds for unrelated code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new or renamed packages. Is the name a short lowercase noun? Does it avoid generic names like util, common, base, helpers, or misc?
baseContent.exampleComment: The package name 'helpers' is too generic. Consider renaming it to reflect what it actually provides, like 'auth' or 'retry'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: A package name should tell you what the package does. If it does not, it probably does too many things.

items.text: Are exported names clear without the package prefix (`http.Client`, not `http.HTTPClient`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Since Go uses the package name as a prefix at every call site, exported names should not stutter with the package name. Use http.Client, not http.HTTPClient.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Stuttering names like widget.NewWidget or http.HTTPServer are redundant and harder to read. The package name already provides context.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look at exported types, functions, and constants. Would the name read naturally with the package prefix? e.g., config.Config is borderline; config.Loader is better.
baseContent.exampleComment: The type 'user.UserService' stutters. Since callers write user.UserService, consider just user.Service.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Read your exported names with the package prefix. If it stutters, shorten the name.

items.text: Are interfaces named with `-er` suffix when they have a single method (`Reader`, `Writer`, `Closer`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Single-method interfaces in Go conventionally use the method name with an -er suffix: Reader, Writer, Closer, Stringer, Formatter.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This naming convention is deeply embedded in the Go ecosystem. Following it makes interfaces instantly recognizable and makes your types composable with the standard library.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new interfaces. If they have a single method, does the interface name follow the -er convention? Multi-method interfaces can use descriptive nouns.
baseContent.exampleComment: The interface has a single Validate method. Following Go convention, consider naming it Validator instead of ValidationChecker.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Single-method interfaces get an -er suffix. It is one of Go's most recognizable conventions.

items.text: Are acronyms consistently cased (`ID`, `HTTP`, `URL`, not `Id`, `Http`, `Url`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In Go, acronyms and initialisms should be consistently all-caps or all-lower: ID not Id, HTTP not Http, URL not Url, XML not Xml.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent acronym casing is one of the most common style nits in Go reviews. The standard library uses ID, HTTP, URL, etc. consistently, and community code should follow suit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for common acronyms in new code: Id, Http, Url, Api, Json, Xml, Sql, Tcp. They should be ID, HTTP, URL, API, JSON, XML, SQL, TCP.
baseContent.exampleComment: Nit: Go convention uses 'ID' not 'Id'. Same for 'URL' not 'Url'. See the Go code review comments wiki.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Acronyms are all-caps in Go: ID, HTTP, URL, API, JSON, XML, SQL.

items.text: Are local variables short but descriptive (`i` for index, `ctx` for context, `err` for error)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go favors short variable names where the scope and context make the meaning clear. Use i for index, ctx for context, err for error, buf for buffer, etc.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Short names reduce visual clutter in a language that already has explicit error handling on every other line. But names should get longer as scope grows wider.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that short-lived variables have short names and that longer-lived or ambiguous variables have more descriptive names.
baseContent.exampleComment: The variable 'currentUserContextObject' could just be 'ctx' or 'userCtx' given the small scope of this function.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Short scope, short name. Long scope, descriptive name.

items.text: Are receiver names short (1-2 chars) and consistent across methods of the same type?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Method receivers in Go should be 1-2 character abbreviations of the type name and consistent across all methods: s for Server, c for Client, r for Repository.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Go convention avoids self or this. Short receiver names keep method bodies concise. Inconsistent receiver names across methods of the same type are confusing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all methods on a type. Are receiver names the same? Are they 1-2 characters? Do they avoid 'self' or 'this'?
baseContent.exampleComment: The receiver name changes from 's' to 'srv' to 'server' across methods of the same type. Pick one short name and use it everywhere.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick a 1-2 letter receiver name and use it consistently across all methods of the type.

items.text: Are unexported names used for package-internal types and functions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Types, functions, and variables that are only used within a package should be unexported (lowercase first letter). Only export what is part of the package's public API.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exporting everything inflates the API surface, makes breaking changes more likely, and confuses consumers about what is stable vs internal. Unexported names are a contract that the symbol is private.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new exported names. Is each one intentionally public? Could any be unexported without breaking external callers?
baseContent.exampleComment: The function ProcessInternal is exported but only used within this package. Consider making it unexported: processInternal.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Export only what consumers need. Everything else should be unexported.

items.text: Is `MixedCaps` used (not `snake_case` or `SCREAMING_SNAKE`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go uses MixedCaps (or mixedCaps for unexported) for multiword names. Snake_case and SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE are not idiomatic Go.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The Go community has a strong convention around MixedCaps. Using snake_case immediately marks code as non-idiomatic and makes it look out of place in the ecosystem.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for underscores in identifier names. The only acceptable use is in test function names (Test_Foo) and CGo. Constants should also be MixedCaps, not SCREAMING_SNAKE.
baseContent.exampleComment: Nit: Go convention uses MixedCaps, not snake_case. 'max_retry_count' should be 'maxRetryCount'.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: MixedCaps for everything. No underscores in Go identifiers (except test functions and CGo).

items.text: Does each package have a clear, single purpose?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each Go package should have a focused purpose. A package should provide a cohesive set of related functionality, not be a grab-bag of unrelated utilities.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Focused packages are easier to understand, test, and reuse. They also lead to cleaner dependency graphs and avoid circular imports.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the package's exported API. Can you summarize its purpose in one sentence? If not, it may need to be split.
baseContent.exampleComment: This package handles both authentication and email sending. These are distinct concerns. Consider splitting into 'auth' and 'email' packages.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One purpose per package. If you cannot describe it in one sentence, it is doing too much.

items.text: Are files within a package organized logically (not one giant file)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Files within a package should be organized by topic or concern, not shoved into a single massive file. Go does not require one-file-per-type, but logical grouping helps.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A 3000-line file is hard to navigate. Splitting by concern (types, handlers, middleware, store) makes it easier for developers to find what they need.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if any file in the package is excessively long. Are related types and functions grouped together in files with descriptive names?
baseContent.exampleComment: This server.go file is over 2000 lines. Consider splitting handlers, middleware, and routes into separate files.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Organize files by concern within a package. Avoid monolithic files.

items.text: Is the `internal/` directory used to prevent external packages from importing internal code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The internal/ directory in Go prevents external packages from importing its contents. The Go compiler enforces this boundary.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without internal/, other teams or modules may depend on implementation details you intended to be private. The internal/ directory provides a compiler-enforced privacy boundary.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if there are packages that should not be imported externally. Are they under internal/? Is anything in internal/ that should be public?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'transform' package contains implementation details of the pipeline. Consider moving it to internal/transform to prevent external imports.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use internal/ to enforce package privacy at the compiler level.

items.text: Are `cmd/` packages used for binaries and kept thin (parse flags, call into libraries)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Binary entry points should live in cmd/<name>/main.go and be kept thin. They parse flags, set up dependencies, and call into library code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Putting business logic in main makes it untestable. Thin cmd packages allow the same library code to be used by multiple binaries, tests, and tools.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the main package contains business logic. Does it do more than parse flags, wire dependencies, and call a Run or Serve function?
baseContent.exampleComment: The main.go file contains business logic for processing orders. Consider moving that into an internal/orders package so it can be tested and reused.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep cmd/ thin. Parse flags, wire dependencies, and delegate to library code.

items.text: Is the project layout consistent with the team's conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The project should follow a consistent directory layout that the team has agreed upon. Common patterns include cmd/, internal/, pkg/, api/, etc.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent layout reduces cognitive load when switching between projects. New team members ramp up faster when they know where to find things.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare the directory structure with the team's conventions. Are new packages placed in the expected locations?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new 'notification' package is at the repo root, but our convention puts all service packages under internal/. Could you move it?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow your team's layout conventions. Consistency matters more than any particular structure.

items.text: Are init() functions avoided unless absolutely necessary?

baseContent.whatItMeans: init() functions run automatically when a package is imported, which makes behavior implicit, hard to test, and order-dependent. They should be avoided unless truly necessary.
baseContent.whyItMatters: init() functions create hidden side effects on import. They make testing harder because you cannot control when they run. They also create implicit dependencies between packages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for func init() in the diff. Is there a compelling reason it cannot be an explicit initialization call?
baseContent.exampleComment: This init() function registers the handler. Could this be done explicitly in main() instead? It would be easier to test and makes the dependency visible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer explicit initialization over init(). Make side effects visible.

items.text: Is the code straightforward and readable ("Clear is better than clever")?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go favors clarity over cleverness. Code should be easy to read, understand, and maintain. Avoid unnecessary abstractions, magic, or one-liners that require mental gymnastics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code is read far more often than it is written. Clear code reduces bugs, speeds up reviews, and makes onboarding easier. 'Clear is better than clever' is a core Go proverb.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the code. Can a mid-level Go developer understand it without extra context? Are there clever tricks that could be replaced with straightforward logic?
baseContent.exampleComment: This chain of reflect calls is clever but hard to follow. Could we use a type switch instead? It would be longer but much more readable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write code for the reader, not to impress. Clear is better than clever.

items.text: Are small interfaces preferred over large ones ("The bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction")?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go interfaces should be small, ideally one or two methods. The bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction. The standard library's most powerful interfaces (Reader, Writer, Stringer) have a single method.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small interfaces are easier to implement, mock, and compose. Large interfaces couple implementations tightly and make testing burdensome.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new interfaces. Do they have more than 3-4 methods? Could they be split into smaller, composable interfaces?
baseContent.exampleComment: This Repository interface has 12 methods. Could it be split into smaller interfaces like Reader, Writer, Deleter? Callers can embed only what they need.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
type UserStore interface {
    Create(ctx context.Context, u *User) error
    Update(ctx context.Context, u *User) error
    Delete(ctx context.Context, id string) error
    Get(ctx context.Context, id string) (*User, error)
    List(ctx context.Context, filter Filter) ([]*User, error)
    Count(ctx context.Context) (int, error)
    Search(ctx context.Context, q string) ([]*User, error)
}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: The bigger the interface, the weaker the abstraction. Keep interfaces small and composable.

items.text: Is `interface{}` / `any` usage minimized in favor of concrete types or constrained generics?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The empty interface (interface{} or any) accepts any type but provides no compile-time guarantees. It should be used sparingly, with concrete types or constrained generics preferred.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using any loses type safety. Callers must use type assertions, which can panic at runtime. Generics (Go 1.18+) often provide a better solution for type-flexible code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for interface{} or any in function signatures. Could the parameter be a concrete type, an interface with methods, or a generic type constraint?
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accepts 'any' for the payload. Since all callers pass a struct, could we define an interface with the methods we actually use?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Minimize any/interface{}. Use concrete types, interfaces with methods, or generics instead.

items.text: Are interfaces defined by the consumer, not the implementer?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In Go, interfaces should be defined where they are used (by the consumer), not where they are implemented (by the provider). This decouples packages.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When the implementer defines the interface, consumers are forced to depend on the implementer's package. When consumers define their own interface, they depend only on the methods they need.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if interfaces are defined next to their implementations or next to their consumers. Are callers importing a package just for an interface?
baseContent.exampleComment: The UserStore interface is defined in the 'store' package alongside the implementation. Consider defining it in the 'handler' package where it is consumed, with only the methods the handler needs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define interfaces at the call site, not the implementation site. Accept interfaces, return structs.

items.text: Is the zero value of types useful and safe?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go types should be designed so that their zero value is valid and useful without explicit initialization. sync.Mutex, bytes.Buffer, and http.ServeMux all work at zero value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A useful zero value means less boilerplate for users. They can declare a variable and start using it without calling a constructor. It also prevents nil-pointer panics.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new struct types. What happens if someone declares var x MyType and starts calling methods? Will it panic or produce incorrect results?
baseContent.exampleComment: The Config struct panics when used without calling NewConfig() because the internal map is nil. Consider initializing it lazily in the getter so the zero value is safe.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design types so the zero value is ready to use. It is one of Go's most powerful conventions.

items.text: Is reflection avoided unless truly necessary?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The reflect package should be avoided unless truly necessary (e.g., serialization libraries, ORM frameworks). Most application code does not need reflection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reflection is slow, bypasses compile-time type safety, and produces code that is hard to read and debug. Generics (Go 1.18+) now cover many cases that previously required reflection.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for reflect. in the diff. Is there a non-reflect alternative? Could generics, interfaces, or code generation solve the problem?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses reflect to copy struct fields. Since both types have the same field names, consider a simple manual mapping function. It is more verbose but much clearer and faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid reflect. Use interfaces, generics, or code generation instead.

items.text: Are all errors checked? (`err` not silently discarded with `_`)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every function that returns an error must have that error checked. Assigning an error to _ (blank identifier) silently discards it, hiding failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unchecked errors are one of the most common sources of bugs in Go. A silently discarded error can lead to nil pointer dereferences, corrupt data, or cascading failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for '_ =' in the diff. Check that every function call that returns an error has the error handled (returned, logged, or explicitly justified).
baseContent.exampleComment: The error from db.Close() is discarded with _. Even if we cannot recover, we should log it so we know when cleanup fails.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
data, _ := json.Marshal(user)
w.Write(data)
_ = db.Close()

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never discard errors with _. Check every error, even in cleanup code.

items.text: Are errors returned, not panicked on? (`panic` only for truly unrecoverable situations)

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go functions should return errors as values rather than panicking. panic() should only be used for truly unrecoverable situations like programmer errors or impossible states.
baseContent.whyItMatters: panic() unwinds the stack and crashes the goroutine (or the whole program). Unlike returned errors, panics are hard to handle gracefully and can bring down production services.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for panic( in the diff. Is each panic truly for an unrecoverable situation? Could it be an error return instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This panic on invalid configuration could be an error return. The caller can then decide how to handle it rather than crashing.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Return errors, do not panic. Reserve panic for genuinely impossible states.

items.text: Are errors wrapped with context using `fmt.Errorf("doing X: %w", err)`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When returning an error from a called function, wrap it with context using fmt.Errorf("doing X: %w", err) so the error chain tells a complete story.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A bare 'permission denied' error is useless. 'open config /etc/app.conf: permission denied' tells you exactly what failed. Wrapping builds a breadcrumb trail through the call stack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error returns in the diff. Are errors being returned bare (return err) or with context (return fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err))?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns the raw error from os.Open. Could you wrap it with context? e.g., fmt.Errorf("opening user data file %s: %w", path, err)

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always wrap errors with context. The error message should tell you what operation failed.

items.text: Is the `%w` verb used (not `%v` or `%s`) when the caller needs to unwrap the error?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When wrapping errors with fmt.Errorf, use %w (not %v or %s) to preserve the error chain. Only %w allows callers to use errors.Is() and errors.As() on the wrapped error.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using %v or %s formats the error as a string, breaking the error chain. Callers can no longer programmatically inspect or match the underlying error.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for fmt.Errorf in the diff. Is %w used for error arguments? Are there cases using %v or %s with errors?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses %v to wrap the error, which breaks the error chain. Please use %w so callers can still use errors.Is() to match the underlying error.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use %w to wrap errors and preserve the chain. Use %v only when you intentionally want to hide the underlying error.

items.text: Are error messages lowercase and without trailing punctuation (Go convention)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go convention says error messages should be lowercase and not end with punctuation. This is because errors are often wrapped, and a capital letter or period in the middle of a chain looks wrong.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When errors are chained (e.g., 'opening config: Reading file: Permission denied.'), inconsistent casing and punctuation create a messy, hard-to-read message.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error strings in fmt.Errorf and errors.New calls. Are they lowercase? Do they lack trailing punctuation?
baseContent.exampleComment: Nit: Go convention is lowercase error messages without trailing punctuation. 'Failed to connect to database.' should be 'connecting to database' (also prefer the -ing form for wrapping).

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
return fmt.Errorf("Failed to open file: %w", err)
return errors.New("Connection timed out.")

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
return fmt.Errorf("opening file: %w", err)
return errors.New("connection timed out")

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Error messages: lowercase, no trailing punctuation, describe the failed operation.

items.text: Are error messages specific enough to locate the problem ("open config file /etc/app.conf: permission denied")?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Error messages should include enough context to locate the problem without debugging. Include relevant identifiers, file paths, or parameter values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A vague 'not found' error is almost useless in production. 'user 12345 not found in tenant abc' tells you exactly what went wrong and where to look.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read error messages in the diff. Could you diagnose the issue from the error alone? Are key identifiers included?
baseContent.exampleComment: The error message 'invalid input' does not help debugging. Could you include which field is invalid and what value was received?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Error messages should be specific enough to diagnose the problem without a debugger.

items.text: Are sentinel errors (`var ErrNotFound = errors.New(...)`) used for expected, matchable conditions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Sentinel errors are package-level variables like var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found") that callers can match with errors.Is(). They should be used for expected, well-defined error conditions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sentinel errors give callers a clean way to handle specific error cases without string matching. They form part of the package's API contract.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if callers need to distinguish between error types. Are sentinel errors defined for those cases? Are they prefixed with Err?
baseContent.exampleComment: Callers need to check if a user was not found vs. a database error. Consider defining var ErrUserNotFound = errors.New("user not found") as a sentinel.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define sentinel errors for expected conditions callers need to match. Always use the Err prefix.

items.text: Are custom error types used when callers need to extract structured information?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When callers need more than just an error match - like extracting an HTTP status code, field name, or retry delay - define a custom error type that implements the error interface.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Custom error types let callers extract structured information using errors.As(). This is more robust than parsing error strings and allows richer error handling strategies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if callers need to extract data from errors. Is a custom type defined? Does it implement Error() string? Can callers use errors.As() to extract it?
baseContent.exampleComment: The handler needs to extract the HTTP status code from this error. Consider a custom error type with a StatusCode field so the middleware can use errors.As().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use custom error types when callers need to extract structured information from errors.

items.text: Is `errors.Is()` and `errors.As()` used instead of direct comparison or type assertion?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use errors.Is() to check for sentinel errors and errors.As() to extract custom error types. Do not use == for error comparison or direct type assertions, as they do not traverse wrapped error chains.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Direct comparison (err == ErrNotFound) fails if the error was wrapped with fmt.Errorf("%w"). errors.Is() unwraps the chain. Similarly, errors.As() unwraps to find the target type.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for error comparisons using == or type assertions on error values. Should they be errors.Is() or errors.As() instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses err == sql.ErrNoRows which will fail if the error was wrapped. Use errors.Is(err, sql.ErrNoRows) to handle wrapped errors.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use errors.Is() for sentinel checks and errors.As() for type extraction. They handle wrapped errors correctly.

items.text: Are errors handled once (either logged or returned, not both)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: An error should be either logged or returned, not both. Logging and returning the same error causes duplicate log entries as the error bubbles up the stack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When every layer logs the error, you get five log lines for one failure, making it hard to correlate and debug. The top-level handler should be responsible for logging.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error handling blocks. Is the same error both logged and returned? Only the final handler should log; intermediate layers should wrap and return.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function logs the error and also returns it. The caller will log it again. Pick one: either log it here and return a different error, or just return it with context.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle errors once: intermediate layers wrap and return; the top-level handler logs.

items.text: Is the happy path left-aligned (guard clauses return early on error)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The successful execution path should be at the left edge of the function. Error cases should be handled with early returns (guard clauses), keeping the main logic unindented.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Left-aligned happy paths are easier to scan. Deep nesting from if-else chains makes code harder to read and reason about.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the main logic is inside nested if-else blocks. Could error checks be flipped to early returns to flatten the code?
baseContent.exampleComment: The success logic is nested three levels deep. Consider flipping the error checks to early returns so the happy path stays at the left margin.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle errors early and return. Keep the happy path left-aligned and unindented.

items.text: Are multi-return values `(result, error)` — never `(error, result)`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go functions that can fail should return (result, error), with the error as the last return value. Never return (error, result).
baseContent.whyItMatters: The (result, error) convention is universal in Go. Violating it confuses every Go developer who reads your code and breaks expectations set by the standard library.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function signatures. Is the error always the last return value? Are there functions returning error as the first value?
baseContent.exampleComment: This function returns (error, *User) but Go convention is (*User, error) with the error last.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Error is always the last return value. No exceptions.

items.text: Are errors from deferred calls handled (`defer f.Close()` — does `Close` return an error that matters)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: defer f.Close() discards the error from Close(). For writable resources (files, database transactions), a Close error may mean data was not flushed. These errors should be captured.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Ignoring Close() errors on writable files can lead to silent data loss. The data may be buffered and the flush during Close() could fail.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check defer statements. Are they closing writable resources? Is the error from Close being handled, especially for files opened for writing and database transactions?
baseContent.exampleComment: This defers f.Close() but we are writing to the file. If Close fails, written data may be lost. Consider capturing the Close error.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: For writable resources, capture the error from deferred Close. Data loss can be silent.

items.text: Are cleanup operations in `defer` statements idempotent?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Deferred cleanup functions should be safe to call multiple times. If a resource might be closed in the normal flow and also in a defer, the defer must handle the double-close gracefully.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Double-closing a file descriptor or channel causes panics or undefined behavior. Idempotent cleanup prevents these bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check defer cleanup patterns. Could the deferred function be called after the resource was already cleaned up in the normal flow?
baseContent.exampleComment: The transaction is committed in the happy path but also rolled back in the defer. If Commit succeeds, the deferred Rollback will fail. Consider checking if the transaction was already committed.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Deferred cleanup must be safe to call even if the resource was already cleaned up.

items.text: Is every goroutine launched with a clear shutdown path?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every goroutine that is started must have a defined way to stop. Without a shutdown mechanism, goroutines leak and consume memory indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Goroutine leaks are a common source of memory leaks in Go services. A leaked goroutine holds onto its stack, any referenced memory, and may hold open connections or file handles.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each 'go' statement, trace how the goroutine terminates. Is there a context cancellation, done channel, or WaitGroup that ensures it stops?
baseContent.exampleComment: This goroutine runs an infinite for-select but there is no case for context cancellation or a done signal. How does it stop when the server shuts down?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every goroutine needs a clear exit path. If you cannot explain how it stops, it is a leak.

items.text: Are goroutine leaks avoided? Is there a mechanism to stop every goroutine (`context.Context`, `done` channel, `sync.WaitGroup`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Goroutine leaks occur when goroutines are started but never terminated. This happens when a goroutine blocks forever on a channel, a missing context cancellation, or a forgotten WaitGroup.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each leaked goroutine consumes at least 2KB of stack space, plus any heap memory it references. Over time, this causes OOM kills in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all go statements. Is there a context.Context, done channel, or sync.WaitGroup that ensures the goroutine exits? Run tests with goleak.
baseContent.exampleComment: If sendNotification is slow, this goroutine could pile up unboundedly. Consider using a worker pool with a bounded number of goroutines.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every goroutine must have a guaranteed exit. Use goleak in tests to catch leaks.

items.text: Are goroutines not launched in library code without the caller's knowledge?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Library code should not spawn goroutines without the caller's knowledge or control. The caller should decide on concurrency, not the library.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hidden goroutines are impossible for the caller to manage. They cannot be cancelled, waited on, or bounded. This leads to resource leaks and makes the library harder to use in different contexts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check library functions. Do they spawn goroutines internally? If so, does the caller have a way to control or wait for them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This library function spawns a goroutine internally. The caller has no way to wait for it or cancel it. Consider accepting a callback or returning a channel instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Libraries should not spawn goroutines. Let the caller control concurrency.

items.text: Is `go func()` capturing loop variables correctly (Go < 1.22 pitfall)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In Go versions before 1.22, goroutines launched inside a loop capture the loop variable by reference, not by value. All goroutines end up using the last value of the variable. Go 1.22+ fixed this.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This is one of the most common concurrency bugs in Go. It leads to race conditions and incorrect behavior where all goroutines process the same (last) element.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for go func() inside range loops. If targeting Go < 1.22, is the loop variable passed as a parameter to the goroutine function?
baseContent.exampleComment: This goroutine captures the loop variable 'item' by reference. In Go < 1.22, all goroutines will see the last value. Pass it as a parameter: go func(item Item) {...}(item).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pass loop variables as goroutine function parameters (essential for Go < 1.22, good practice for all versions).

items.text: Are `sync.WaitGroup` counters incremented before `go` statements, not inside goroutines?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.WaitGroup.Add() must be called before the go statement, not inside the goroutine. Otherwise, wg.Wait() might return before the goroutine has a chance to call Add().
baseContent.whyItMatters: If Add is called inside the goroutine, there is a race between the goroutine's Add and the main goroutine's Wait. Wait could return prematurely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check WaitGroup usage. Is Add() called in the launching goroutine before the go statement? Not inside the launched goroutine?
baseContent.exampleComment: wg.Add(1) is inside the goroutine. This is a race condition - wg.Wait() could return before the goroutine calls Add. Move wg.Add(1) before the go statement.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always call wg.Add() before the go statement, never inside the goroutine.

items.text: Is the number of goroutines bounded (worker pool pattern, semaphore)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Spawning unbounded goroutines (e.g., one per request or per item) can exhaust memory and CPU. Use worker pools, semaphores, or rate limiters to bound concurrency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A million goroutines each taking 2-8KB of stack is 2-8GB of memory. Unbounded goroutines can also overwhelm downstream services and databases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if goroutines are spawned proportionally to input size or request rate. Is there a bound? A worker pool? A semaphore?
baseContent.exampleComment: This spawns one goroutine per item in the slice. For large inputs, this could exhaust memory. Consider a worker pool with a bounded number of workers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Bound your goroutines. Use worker pools or semaphores to limit concurrency.

items.text: Are channels used for communication, mutexes for state protection?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Channels are for passing data and signaling between goroutines. Mutexes are for protecting shared state. Using channels as mutexes (or vice versa) leads to convoluted code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Choosing the right synchronization primitive makes code clearer. Channels excel at producer-consumer patterns and signaling. Mutexes excel at protecting a shared data structure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if channels are being used to guard access to shared data (mutex territory) or if mutexes are used to coordinate goroutines (channel territory).
baseContent.exampleComment: This channel-based approach to guard a map is more complex than a simple sync.Mutex. Since there is no producer-consumer pattern here, a mutex would be clearer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Share memory by communicating (channels), protect shared memory with mutexes. Use the right tool.

items.text: Is the channel direction specified in function signatures (`chan<-`, `<-chan`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Function parameters should specify channel direction: chan<- T for send-only, <-chan T for receive-only. This makes the function's role clear and catches misuse at compile time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Channel direction constraints prevent bugs at compile time. A producer function should not accidentally receive from its output channel, and vice versa.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function signatures that accept channels. Do they specify direction (chan<-, <-chan)? Bidirectional chan should only be used when both send and receive are needed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function only sends to the channel but the parameter is bidirectional. Use chan<- Result to make the intent clear and catch misuse at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Specify channel direction in function signatures. Let the compiler catch misuse.

items.text: Are channels closed by the sender, never the receiver?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Channels should be closed by the sender (producer), never by the receiver (consumer). Sending on a closed channel panics, so the closer must be the one who knows when sending is done.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a receiver closes the channel, the sender will panic on the next send. The sender is the only one who knows when there is no more data to send.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check who closes each channel. Is it always the sender? Are receivers never calling close()?
baseContent.exampleComment: The consumer goroutine closes the channel, but the producer may still be sending. This will panic. Move the close() to the producer side.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only the sender closes a channel. Receivers should never close.

items.text: Are channels not closed more than once?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Closing an already-closed channel panics in Go. You must ensure a channel is closed exactly once.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A double-close panic can crash your service. This often happens when multiple goroutines might close the same channel or when shutdown logic races.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check channel close() calls. Is there any scenario where the same channel could be closed twice? Are there multiple goroutines that might close it?
baseContent.exampleComment: Both the timeout and the completion paths close the done channel. If both fire, the second close will panic. Use sync.Once to ensure it is closed exactly once.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never close a channel twice. Use sync.Once if multiple goroutines might close the same channel.

items.text: Are buffered channels used only when the buffer size is justified?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buffered channels should only be used when you have a clear reason for the buffer size. Unbuffered channels provide synchronization guarantees that buffered channels do not.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A buffer of 100 chosen arbitrarily can mask backpressure problems, lead to message loss on shutdown, and make capacity planning harder. Unbuffered channels make flow control explicit.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check make(chan T, N) calls. Is the buffer size N justified? Is there a comment explaining why buffered is needed?
baseContent.exampleComment: The buffer size of 100 seems arbitrary. What happens when it fills up? Consider an unbuffered channel with proper backpressure or document why 100 is the right size.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Arbitrary buffer, no justification
ch := make(chan Event, 100)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to unbuffered channels. Buffer only when you can justify the size.

items.text: Is `select` with `default` used intentionally (non-blocking operations)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: select with a default case makes channel operations non-blocking. This should be intentional, not accidental. A default case can cause busy-spinning if not careful.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An accidental default case in a select loop creates a busy spin that consumes 100% CPU. Non-blocking selects have valid uses (try-send, try-receive) but must be deliberate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check select blocks with default cases. Is the non-blocking behavior intentional? Could it spin in a loop?
baseContent.exampleComment: This select has a default case inside a for loop, which will busy-spin. Remove the default to block until a channel is ready, or add a sleep/ticker.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: select with default is non-blocking. Use it deliberately for try-send/try-receive, not in loops.

items.text: Is `select` with `context.Done()` used for cancellation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Long-running or blocking operations should include a case for <-ctx.Done() in their select statements to support cancellation and shutdown.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without ctx.Done() handling, goroutines cannot be cancelled and will block until the operation completes (which might be forever). This causes goroutine leaks during shutdown.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check select statements in long-running goroutines. Is there a ctx.Done() case? Can the goroutine be cancelled?
baseContent.exampleComment: This select blocks on the channel but has no ctx.Done() case. If the context is cancelled (e.g., during shutdown), this goroutine will leak.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always include ctx.Done() in select statements for long-running goroutines.

items.text: Is `sync.Mutex` preferred over `sync.RWMutex` unless read-heavy access is proven?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.RWMutex has higher overhead than sync.Mutex. Only use RWMutex when you have proven that read-heavy workloads benefit from concurrent reads.
baseContent.whyItMatters: RWMutex is not a free upgrade. It has more complex locking logic and higher overhead per operation. For workloads that are not heavily read-dominant, Mutex is faster.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for sync.RWMutex usage. Is there evidence that reads vastly outnumber writes? Has it been benchmarked? Would a plain Mutex be simpler?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses RWMutex but the write frequency seems similar to reads. Have we benchmarked this? A plain Mutex might be simpler and equally fast.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start with sync.Mutex. Only use RWMutex when benchmarks prove read-heavy access benefits from it.

items.text: Are mutexes not copied (embed a pointer or use pointer receivers)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A sync.Mutex (or any sync type) must not be copied after first use. Copying a locked mutex creates a second locked mutex that can never be unlocked.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Copying a mutex leads to deadlocks and data races. This can happen subtly when a struct containing a mutex is passed by value instead of by pointer.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if structs containing sync.Mutex are passed by value. Do methods use pointer receivers? Is go vet clean (it detects this)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This struct contains a sync.Mutex but some methods use value receivers. This copies the mutex on every call, which can deadlock. Use pointer receivers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never copy a mutex. Use pointer receivers on types that contain sync primitives.

items.text: Is `sync.Once` used for one-time initialization?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.Once guarantees a function runs exactly once, even when called from multiple goroutines. Use it for lazy initialization of singletons, connections, etc.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without sync.Once, lazy initialization requires manual locking and double-checked locking patterns that are error-prone. sync.Once is simple, correct, and fast after the first call.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for lazy initialization patterns. Is sync.Once used? Are there DIY double-checked locking patterns that could be replaced with sync.Once?
baseContent.exampleComment: This manual double-checked locking for the DB connection could be simplified with sync.Once. It handles all the synchronization correctly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use sync.Once for one-time initialization. It is simple, correct, and efficient.

items.text: Is `sync.Map` only used for its intended use cases (append-only, disjoint keys)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.Map is optimized for two specific patterns: (1) append-only maps where keys are only written once, and (2) maps where each goroutine accesses a disjoint set of keys. For general-purpose concurrent maps, use a regular map with sync.RWMutex.
baseContent.whyItMatters: sync.Map has higher overhead than map+RWMutex for general workloads. It also does not support len(), iteration guarantees, or type safety.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check sync.Map usage. Does the access pattern match one of the two optimized cases? Would a map with a mutex be simpler and faster?
baseContent.exampleComment: This sync.Map is used as a general-purpose cache with frequent reads and writes to the same keys. A regular map with sync.RWMutex would be simpler and likely faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: sync.Map is specialized. For most concurrent maps, use a regular map with a mutex.

items.text: Are race conditions tested with `-race` flag?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go's race detector (-race flag) should be enabled in CI to catch data races. It instruments memory accesses and detects concurrent reads/writes without synchronization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data races cause undefined behavior, corrupted data, and intermittent crashes that are extremely hard to debug. The race detector catches them deterministically during test runs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration. Is go test -race enabled? Are there race detector failures being suppressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: Our CI does not appear to run tests with -race. This should be enabled to catch data races early.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
# CI pipeline - no race detection
go test ./...

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI pipeline - with race detection
go test -race -count=1 ./...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always run tests with -race in CI. Data races are undefined behavior.

items.text: Is `sync.Pool` used correctly (objects may be garbage collected, not for connection pooling)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.Pool is for reusing short-lived, frequently allocated objects to reduce GC pressure. Objects in the pool may be garbage collected at any time. It is not for connection pooling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Misusing sync.Pool (e.g., for connection pooling) leads to bugs because pooled objects can be reclaimed at any GC. It is only suitable for reducing allocation pressure in hot paths.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check sync.Pool usage. Is it used for short-lived, frequently allocated objects? Is there any assumption about objects staying in the pool?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses sync.Pool for database connections, but pooled objects can be garbage collected at any time. Use sql.DB's built-in connection pool instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: sync.Pool is for reducing GC pressure on short-lived objects. It is not a connection pool.

items.text: Is `context.Context` the first parameter of functions that accept it?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions that accept a context.Context should have it as the first parameter, conventionally named ctx. Do not bury it in the middle of the parameter list.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This is a universal Go convention. Every Go developer expects context as the first parameter. Violating this makes your API surprising and harder to use.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function signatures that accept context.Context. Is it the first parameter? Is it named ctx?
baseContent.exampleComment: The context parameter is the third argument. Go convention puts it first: func GetUser(ctx context.Context, id string) (*User, error).

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: func GetUser(id string, db *sql.DB, ctx context.Context) (*User, error) { ... }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: func GetUser(ctx context.Context, id string) (*User, error) { ... }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Context is always the first parameter, always named ctx.

items.text: Are contexts not stored in structs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: context.Context should be passed as a function parameter, not stored in a struct field. Contexts are request-scoped and should flow through the call stack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Storing a context in a struct ties the struct's lifetime to a specific request. If the struct outlives the request, the context may be cancelled, causing confusing errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check struct definitions for context.Context fields. Should the context be passed per-call instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: Storing the context in the Client struct means all calls share one context. If it is cancelled, all future calls fail. Pass context per-call instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pass context through function parameters, never store it in structs.

items.text: Is `context.Background()` used only at the top level (main, init, tests)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: context.Background() creates a non-cancellable root context. It should only be used in main(), init(), tests, and at the top of a request chain.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using context.Background() deep in the call stack means the operation cannot be cancelled by the caller. This defeats the purpose of context propagation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for context.Background() in the diff. Is it only at top-level entry points? Are there cases where a parent context should be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses context.Background() inside a service method. The handler already has a request context - pass it through instead so the operation can be cancelled.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: context.Background() belongs in main, init, and tests. Everywhere else, propagate the caller's context.

items.text: Is `context.TODO()` not left in production code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: context.TODO() is a placeholder for when you know you need a context but have not yet decided which one to use. It should not be left in production code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: context.TODO() signals incomplete work. Like a TODO comment, it should be resolved before merge. It creates a non-cancellable context that bypasses shutdown signals.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for context.TODO() in the diff. Is there a plan to replace it? Should it be context.Background() (top-level) or a propagated context?
baseContent.exampleComment: This context.TODO() should be replaced with the request context from the handler. It currently bypasses cancellation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Resolve context.TODO() before merging. It is a placeholder, not a solution.

items.text: Are context cancellation functions called (typically with `defer cancel()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When creating a derived context with context.WithCancel, WithTimeout, or WithDeadline, the returned cancel function must be called to release resources. Typically use defer cancel().
baseContent.whyItMatters: Not calling the cancel function leaks the context's resources (timers, goroutines) until the parent is cancelled. This causes memory leaks in long-running services.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for context.WithCancel, WithTimeout, and WithDeadline. Is the cancel function called? Is defer cancel() used?
baseContent.exampleComment: The cancel function from context.WithTimeout is never called. Add defer cancel() immediately after creating the context.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always defer cancel() when creating derived contexts. Leaking contexts leaks resources.

items.text: Are context values used sparingly and only for request-scoped data (not for function parameters)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: context.WithValue should only carry request-scoped data that crosses API boundaries (trace IDs, auth tokens, request IDs). It should not replace function parameters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Context values are untyped, invisible in function signatures, and hard to test. Overusing them turns the context into a grab-bag of hidden dependencies.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for context.WithValue usage. Is the data truly request-scoped? Could it be a function parameter instead? Are unexported keys used to avoid collisions?
baseContent.exampleComment: Passing the database connection via context.WithValue hides a dependency. This should be a function parameter or struct field, not a context value.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Context values are for request-scoped metadata only. Use function parameters for dependencies.

items.text: Are `defer` statements used for cleanup (closing files, releasing locks, closing response bodies)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: defer should be used immediately after acquiring a resource to ensure cleanup happens regardless of how the function exits (normal return, error, panic).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without defer, cleanup code can be missed on early returns or panics. defer guarantees cleanup in LIFO order when the function returns.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check resource acquisition points. Is defer used for closing files, releasing locks, closing response bodies, and rolling back transactions?
baseContent.exampleComment: The mutex is locked but the unlock is at the end of the function instead of using defer. If an early return is added later, the mutex will not be released.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Acquire a resource, immediately defer its cleanup. This pattern prevents resource leaks.

items.text: Is `defer` not used in long-running loops (resources won't be released until function returns)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: defer runs when the enclosing function returns, not when the loop iteration ends. In a loop, deferred resources accumulate and are not released until the function exits.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Processing 10,000 files in a loop with defer file.Close() keeps all 10,000 file handles open until the function returns, potentially exhausting file descriptors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for defer inside loops. Are resources accumulating? Should the loop body be extracted to a helper function so defer runs per iteration?
baseContent.exampleComment: This defer inside the loop means all files stay open until processAll returns. Extract the body to a helper function so defer runs per file.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Defer in loops accumulates resources. Extract loop bodies to helper functions.

items.text: Are `http.Response.Body` always closed, even when the response is an error?

baseContent.whatItMeans: http.Response.Body must always be closed after use, even when the response is an error status. Not closing it leaks the underlying TCP connection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked HTTP connections exhaust the connection pool, causing timeouts and failures under load. The body must be both fully read and closed for connection reuse.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP client calls. Is resp.Body always closed? Is it closed even when the status code indicates an error?
baseContent.exampleComment: The response body is not closed on the error path. Even error responses need their body closed to release the connection.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always close http.Response.Body. Drain it fully for connection reuse.

items.text: Are database connections returned to the pool (rows closed, transactions committed/rolled back)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database rows, statements, and transactions must be closed to return connections to the pool. Forgetting to close rows or commit/rollback a transaction leaks connections.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A leaked database connection reduces the pool's capacity. Under load, this causes connection exhaustion and database timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check database queries. Are rows closed (defer rows.Close())? Are transactions committed or rolled back? Are prepared statements closed?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rows from this query are not closed. Each unclosed rows object holds a database connection. Add defer rows.Close() after the error check.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always defer rows.Close(), commit/rollback transactions, and close prepared statements.

items.text: Are file handles closed after use?

baseContent.whatItMeans: File handles from os.Open, os.Create, etc. must be closed after use. Unclosed file handles leak file descriptors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: File descriptor limits are finite (often 1024 or 65535). Leaked handles cause 'too many open files' errors that bring down services.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for os.Open, os.Create, os.OpenFile calls. Is there a corresponding defer f.Close()?
baseContent.exampleComment: This file opened with os.Create is never closed. Add defer f.Close() after the error check.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Open a file, immediately defer its Close. No exceptions.

items.text: Are temporary files cleaned up?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Temporary files created with os.CreateTemp or os.MkdirTemp should be cleaned up when no longer needed, typically with defer os.Remove.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Temporary files that are not cleaned up accumulate on disk, consuming storage and potentially leaving sensitive data accessible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for os.CreateTemp and os.MkdirTemp calls. Is there cleanup via defer os.Remove or defer os.RemoveAll?
baseContent.exampleComment: This temporary file is created but never removed. Add defer os.Remove(f.Name()) to clean up.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always clean up temporary files with defer os.Remove after creation.

items.text: Is `io.Copy` or `io.ReadAll` used with appropriate size limits to prevent memory exhaustion?

baseContent.whatItMeans: io.ReadAll and io.Copy read until EOF with no size limit. Malicious or buggy input could cause memory exhaustion. Use io.LimitReader to cap the size.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A 10GB request body read with io.ReadAll will consume 10GB of memory. In a web server handling multiple requests, this is a trivial denial of service attack.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for io.ReadAll and io.Copy with untrusted input. Is there a size limit? Is io.LimitReader used?
baseContent.exampleComment: This reads the entire request body with io.ReadAll without a size limit. A malicious client could send gigabytes. Use http.MaxBytesReader or io.LimitReader.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always limit the size of untrusted input when using io.ReadAll or io.Copy.

items.text: Are struct fields ordered to minimize padding (largest to smallest alignment)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Go structs may have padding bytes between fields for alignment. Ordering fields from largest to smallest alignment minimizes wasted space.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Poor field ordering can waste 30-50% of struct memory due to padding. For structs allocated in large quantities, this adds up significantly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check large structs or hot-path structs. Are fields ordered by size (int64/pointers first, then int32, then int16, then bool/byte)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This struct has bool fields between int64 fields, adding 7 bytes of padding each. Reordering by size would save 14 bytes per instance.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Order struct fields from largest to smallest to minimize padding. Use fieldalignment tool to check.

items.text: Are struct literals using field names (not positional)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Struct literals should use named fields (Point{X: 1, Y: 2}) not positional (Point{1, 2}). Positional literals break when fields are reordered or added.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Positional struct literals are fragile. Adding a new field to the struct silently shifts values, causing hard-to-detect bugs. Named fields are self-documenting and order-independent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check struct literals in the diff. Are field names specified? Positional literals are only acceptable for small, stable types like Point{1, 2}.
baseContent.exampleComment: This struct literal uses positional fields. If someone adds a field to the struct, values will silently shift. Please use named fields.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
user := User{"Alice", "alice@example.com", true, 0}
// What do true and 0 mean? Breaks if fields are reordered.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use named fields in struct literals. It prevents bugs and is self-documenting.

items.text: Are pointer vs value receivers chosen consistently and correctly for a given type?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All methods on a type should use either pointer receivers or value receivers, not a mix. Mixing leads to confusing behavior around interface satisfaction and method sets.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing receiver types means some methods modify the original and some do not, which is confusing. It also affects whether a value or pointer satisfies an interface.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all methods on a type. Are they all pointer receivers or all value receivers? Is there a consistent choice?
baseContent.exampleComment: Some methods on User use value receivers and others use pointer receivers. This is confusing. Since some methods modify state, all should use pointer receivers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
type User struct { Name string; Age int }

func (u User) GetName() string { return u.Name }  // value receiver
func (u *User) SetName(n string) { u.Name = n }   // pointer receiver
func (u User) String() string { return u.Name }    // value receiver

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
type User struct { Name string; Age int }

func (u *User) GetName() string { return u.Name }    // pointer
func (u *User) SetName(n string) { u.Name = n }      // pointer
func (u *User) String() string { return u.Name }      // pointer - consistent

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick one receiver type and use it consistently. If any method needs a pointer, use pointers for all.

items.text: Are value receivers used for small, immutable types; pointer receivers for large or mutable types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Value receivers make a copy of the receiver. Use them for small, immutable types where copying is cheap. Use pointer receivers for large types or when methods need to mutate the receiver.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Value receivers are safe for concurrent use (no shared state), but copy the entire struct on each call. For large structs, this is expensive. For small structs, it avoids nil pointer issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check receiver types. Are small, read-only types using value receivers? Are large or mutable types using pointer receivers?
baseContent.exampleComment: This 128-byte struct uses value receivers, which copies 128 bytes on every method call. Since the struct is large, switch to pointer receivers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Value receivers for small immutable types. Pointer receivers for large or mutable types.

items.text: Are exported struct fields intentionally public?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Exported struct fields (capitalized) are part of the public API. Each should be intentionally public. Internal fields should be unexported.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Exported fields cannot be changed without breaking consumers. Once a field is public, you are committed to supporting it. Unexported fields can be changed freely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check exported struct fields. Is each one intentionally public? Could any be unexported without breaking the API?
baseContent.exampleComment: The 'InternalState' field is exported but seems to be an implementation detail. Consider making it unexported (internalState) to keep the API surface small.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
type Client struct {
    BaseURL     string
    HTTPClient  *http.Client
    RetryCount  int
    Mu          sync.Mutex   // internal detail leaked
    Cache       map[string]any // internal detail leaked
}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
type Client struct {
    BaseURL    string        // public: users configure this
    HTTPClient *http.Client  // public: users may customize
    RetryCount int           // public: users configure this
    mu         sync.Mutex    // internal: not part of API
    cache      map[string]any // internal: not part of API
}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only export fields that are part of the public API. Keep implementation details unexported.

items.text: Are struct tags correct and consistent (`json:"fieldName"`, `db:"column_name"`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Struct tags (json, db, yaml, etc.) should be syntactically correct, consistently formatted, and use the right field name conventions for each serialization format.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Malformed struct tags are silently ignored, leading to missing fields in JSON output or database queries. Inconsistent tag names cause confusion.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check struct tags in the diff. Are they syntactically correct (backtick delimited, no spaces around colon)? Are naming conventions correct (camelCase for JSON, snake_case for DB)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The json tag has a space after the colon which makes it invalid. It should be `json:"userName"` not `json: "userName"`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate struct tags. No spaces after colons. Use go vet to catch malformed tags.

items.text: Are slices pre-allocated with `make([]T, 0, expectedCap)` when the size is known or estimable?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When the size of a slice is known or estimable, pre-allocate with make([]T, 0, cap) to avoid repeated reallocations during append.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each reallocation copies the entire slice to a new, larger array. Pre-allocating eliminates these copies and reduces GC pressure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check append loops. Is the size known beforehand? If so, is make([]T, 0, expectedSize) used?
baseContent.exampleComment: We know there are len(users) items. Pre-allocate the result slice with make([]UserDTO, 0, len(users)) to avoid reallocations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pre-allocate slices when you know the size. It avoids repeated allocations.

items.text: Are nil slices preferred over empty slices for "no data" (`var s []int` not `s := []int{}`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For 'no data' cases, prefer nil slices (var s []int) over empty slices (s := []int{}). A nil slice is functionally equivalent to an empty slice for append, len, range, etc.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A nil slice and an empty slice behave the same in almost all cases, but nil is the zero value and requires no allocation. The distinction matters for JSON: nil marshals to null, empty to [].
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for s := []int{} or make([]int, 0) where var s []int would suffice. If JSON null vs [] matters, choose accordingly.
baseContent.exampleComment: This initializes an empty slice with make([]string, 0) but a nil slice (var results []string) would work the same and is more idiomatic.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer nil slices for no data. They work with append, len, and range, and avoid allocation.

items.text: Are maps initialized with `make(map[K]V, expectedSize)` when size is known?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When the expected size of a map is known, initialize with make(map[K]V, size) to pre-allocate the hash table and avoid rehashing during growth.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Maps grow dynamically, and each growth step involves rehashing all entries. Pre-sizing avoids this overhead for large maps.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check map initializations. Is the size known or estimable? If so, is make used with a size hint?
baseContent.exampleComment: We are building a map from a slice of known length. Use make(map[string]User, len(users)) to pre-allocate.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pre-size maps when the count is known to avoid rehashing.

items.text: Are map reads not assumed to be safe for concurrent access (use `sync.RWMutex` or `sync.Map`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Maps in Go are not safe for concurrent access. Concurrent reads and writes (or concurrent writes) cause a fatal runtime panic, not just data corruption.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unlike many languages where concurrent map access merely causes inconsistent reads, Go's runtime actively detects concurrent map access and crashes the program.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if maps are accessed from multiple goroutines. Is there a sync.Mutex or sync.RWMutex protecting access? Is the map used in a handler (concurrent by definition)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This map is read in the HTTP handler (concurrent) but written during startup. If there is any possibility of concurrent write, add a mutex.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Maps are not thread-safe in Go. Protect concurrent access with a mutex or use sync.Map.

items.text: Are slice append side effects understood (shared underlying arrays)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Appending to a slice may or may not create a new underlying array. If two slices share the same backing array, appending to one can overwrite the other's data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This is one of Go's most subtle bugs. Sub-slices share the backing array, so append to one can corrupt another. It is especially dangerous when returning sub-slices from functions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for sub-slicing followed by append. Is the full slice expression (s[lo:hi:hi]) used to limit capacity? Is copy() used when independence is needed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns a sub-slice of the original. If the caller appends to it, the original slice's data may be overwritten. Use copy or the full slice expression.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Sub-slices share the backing array. Use copy or full slice expressions when independence is needed.

items.text: Are `copy()` used when a true copy is needed, not just reassignment?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Assigning a slice (b = a) copies the slice header, not the data. Both variables point to the same backing array. Use copy() when you need an independent copy.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Modifying one slice modifies the other because they share memory. This causes subtle bugs that are hard to trace.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check slice assignments. Is the intent to create an independent copy? If so, is copy() or append([]T{}, src...) used?
baseContent.exampleComment: Assigning original to backup does not copy the data. Changes to original will also change backup. Use copy() for a true independent copy.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
