original := []int{1, 2, 3}
backup := original // shares the same backing array
original[0] = 99
fmt.Println(backup[0]) // prints 99, not 1!

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
original := []int{1, 2, 3}
backup := make([]int, len(original))
copy(backup, original) // independent copy
original[0] = 99
fmt.Println(backup[0]) // prints 1, as expected

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use copy() for independent slice copies. Slice assignment only copies the header.

items.text: Is map iteration order not relied upon (it's randomized)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Map iteration order in Go is intentionally randomized. Code must not rely on maps being iterated in insertion order or any other deterministic order.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Go randomizes map iteration to prevent code from accidentally depending on order. Code that assumes ordering will have inconsistent behavior across runs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for map iterations where order matters (e.g., building output strings, generating configs). Is the output stable across runs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This iterates over the config map to build a command line. The argument order will be different on each run. Sort the keys first for deterministic output.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never rely on map iteration order. Sort keys when deterministic order is needed.

items.text: Are generics used only when they reduce duplication without sacrificing readability?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generics should be used when they eliminate real code duplication without sacrificing readability. They should not be used just because they are available.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Overusing generics makes Go code harder to read, especially for developers new to the feature. Concrete implementations are often clearer and more maintainable.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic functions and types. Would the code be just as clear (or clearer) with a concrete implementation? Is the generic solving real duplication?
baseContent.exampleComment: This generic function is only used with one type. A concrete implementation would be simpler and more readable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use generics to eliminate real duplication. Prefer concrete code when only one type is used.

items.text: Are type constraints as narrow as possible (`comparable`, `constraints.Ordered`, custom interfaces)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generic type constraints should be as specific as possible: comparable, constraints.Ordered, or custom interfaces. Avoid using any when a narrower constraint exists.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Narrow constraints provide better compile-time checking and make the function's requirements clear. any provides no compile-time guarantees.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic type parameters. Is the constraint as narrow as it can be? Could any be replaced with comparable, constraints.Ordered, or a custom interface?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses [T any] but the function calls sort on T. The constraint should be constraints.Ordered or a custom interface with Less().

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the narrowest type constraint possible. It catches bugs at compile time.

items.text: Are generic functions preferred over generic types when possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When only a function needs to be generic (not the whole type), prefer a generic function over a generic type. Generic types add complexity to all methods.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A generic type forces all consumers to specify type parameters. A generic function only requires type parameters at the call site where they are needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic types. Could the type be non-generic with only specific methods or standalone functions being generic?
baseContent.exampleComment: The entire Cache type is generic but only the Get and Set methods need type parameters. Consider a non-generic Cache with generic helper functions.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer generic functions over generic types when the type itself does not need to be parameterized.

items.text: Is the generic code not overengineered (a concrete implementation may be clearer)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generic code should not be written when a concrete implementation would be equally clear and there is no current duplication to eliminate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Premature generalization adds complexity without benefit. YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It) applies strongly to generics. Write generic code when you have three or more concrete implementations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the generic code has multiple callers with different type parameters. If it is only used with one type, it is probably overengineered.
baseContent.exampleComment: This generic Repository[T] is only instantiated with User. A concrete UserRepository would be simpler until we actually need other entity types.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Do not generalize until you have multiple concrete cases. YAGNI applies to generics.

items.text: Is `strings.Builder` used for building strings in loops, not `+` concatenation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When building strings in a loop, use strings.Builder instead of repeated string concatenation with +. Each + creates a new string and copies all previous content.
baseContent.whyItMatters: String concatenation in a loop is O(n^2) because Go strings are immutable. strings.Builder accumulates bytes efficiently with amortized O(1) appends.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for string concatenation in loops (result += ...). Should it be a strings.Builder?
baseContent.exampleComment: This builds a string with += in a loop, which is O(n^2). Use strings.Builder for O(n) performance.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use strings.Builder or strings.Join for building strings. Never concatenate with + in a loop.

items.text: Is `strconv` used instead of `fmt.Sprintf` for simple number-to-string conversions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For simple type conversions (int to string, string to int), use the strconv package. fmt.Sprintf is slower because it parses format strings at runtime.
baseContent.whyItMatters: strconv.Itoa is 3-5x faster than fmt.Sprintf("%d", n) for integer-to-string conversion. In hot paths, this difference matters.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for fmt.Sprintf used only for simple conversions. Could strconv.Itoa, strconv.FormatFloat, or strconv.FormatBool be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses fmt.Sprintf("%d", count) for a simple int-to-string. strconv.Itoa(count) is faster and more idiomatic.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
id := fmt.Sprintf("%d", userID)
active := fmt.Sprintf("%t", isActive)
price := fmt.Sprintf("%f", amount)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
id := strconv.Itoa(userID)
active := strconv.FormatBool(isActive)
price := strconv.FormatFloat(amount, 'f', 2, 64)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use strconv for simple conversions. Reserve fmt.Sprintf for complex formatting.

items.text: Are `time.Duration` values used (not raw integers) for time-related parameters?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Time-related parameters should use time.Duration, not raw integers. A raw integer could be seconds, milliseconds, or nanoseconds - it is ambiguous without the type.
baseContent.whyItMatters: time.Duration is self-documenting. 5 * time.Second is clear. 5000 could be anything. It also enables compile-time type checking against accidental unit mismatches.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function signatures and config values. Are time-related values using time.Duration? Are there raw int timeouts?
baseContent.exampleComment: The timeout parameter is an int in milliseconds. Use time.Duration instead so callers write 5*time.Second instead of guessing the unit.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use time.Duration for all time-related values. It is self-documenting and type-safe.

items.text: Is `time.Since(start)` used instead of `time.Now().Sub(start)`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use time.Since(start) instead of time.Now().Sub(start). They are equivalent but time.Since is more idiomatic and concise.
baseContent.whyItMatters: time.Since is a standard library convenience function that reads more naturally. It is the idiomatic way to measure elapsed time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for time.Now().Sub( in the diff. Should it be time.Since()?
baseContent.exampleComment: Nit: time.Now().Sub(start) can be simplified to time.Since(start).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use time.Since(start) instead of time.Now().Sub(start). It is more idiomatic.

items.text: Are `filepath` functions used for file paths (not `path` or string manipulation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use filepath.Join, filepath.Dir, filepath.Base, etc. for file system paths. Do not use path.Join (for URLs) or string concatenation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: filepath handles OS-specific path separators correctly. String concatenation or path.Join can produce invalid paths on Windows or when paths contain unusual characters.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for string concatenation to build file paths or use of path.Join for filesystem paths. Should they use filepath?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses path.Join for a file system path. Use filepath.Join instead, which handles OS-specific separators correctly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
configPath := dir + "/" + "config.yaml"    // breaks on Windows
configPath := path.Join(dir, "config.yaml") // path is for URLs, not files

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: configPath := filepath.Join(dir, "config.yaml") // OS-aware path handling

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use filepath for file system paths. Use path for URL paths. Never concatenate with /.

items.text: Is `io.Reader`/`io.Writer` used for streaming data rather than loading everything into memory?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For processing data streams, accept io.Reader/io.Writer parameters instead of []byte. This enables streaming without loading entire payloads into memory.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accepting []byte forces the caller to load everything into memory. io.Reader/io.Writer enable streaming, which handles arbitrarily large data with constant memory.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check functions that process data. Do they accept []byte when io.Reader/io.Writer would work? Could they stream instead of buffering?
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accepts []byte, requiring the entire file in memory. Accepting io.Reader would allow streaming and handle files of any size.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Accept io.Reader/io.Writer for data processing. It enables streaming and reduces memory usage.

items.text: Are `bytes.Buffer` or `strings.Reader` used to adapt between string/byte and io interfaces?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use bytes.Buffer and strings.NewReader to adapt between string/[]byte and io.Reader/io.Writer interfaces, especially in tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: These adapters are lightweight and avoid unnecessary copies. They make it easy to test functions that accept io.Reader/io.Writer.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for manual conversion between strings/bytes and io interfaces. Are bytes.Buffer, strings.NewReader, or bytes.NewReader used?
baseContent.exampleComment: This converts the string to []byte then wraps in a bytes.Reader. Use strings.NewReader directly to avoid the copy.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
data := []byte(jsonStr) // unnecessary copy
reader := bytes.NewReader(data)

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use strings.NewReader for string-to-reader and bytes.Buffer for building output.

items.text: Is `encoding/json` using struct tags correctly? Are `omitempty` and `-` used where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: encoding/json struct tags should use proper conventions: omitempty to omit zero values, - to exclude fields, and camelCase field names for JSON.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper tags, JSON output may contain unwanted fields, use wrong names, or expose internal data. omitempty prevents sending null/zero values unnecessarily.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check JSON struct tags. Are field names appropriate for the API? Is omitempty used where zero values should be omitted? Is - used for internal fields?
baseContent.exampleComment: The Password field is included in JSON output. Add `json:"-"` to exclude it from serialization.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use proper JSON struct tags: camelCase names, omitempty for optional fields, - to exclude.

items.text: Is `net/http` client using a custom `Transport` with timeouts, not the `DefaultClient`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Do not use http.DefaultClient for production HTTP calls. It has no timeouts, which means requests can hang indefinitely. Create a custom client with appropriate timeouts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A request to a slow server with no timeout will block a goroutine forever, eventually exhausting resources. Production HTTP clients must have timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for http.Get, http.Post, or http.DefaultClient usage. Should these use a custom client with timeouts?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses http.DefaultClient which has no timeout. Create a custom client with a timeout to prevent hanging requests.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use http.DefaultClient in production. Always set timeouts.

items.text: Are `sort.Slice` / `slices.Sort` used for sorting?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use sort.Slice, sort.SliceStable, or the newer slices.SortFunc (Go 1.21+) for sorting. Avoid implementing sort.Interface for simple cases.
baseContent.whyItMatters: sort.Slice is concise and avoids the boilerplate of implementing Len, Less, and Swap. slices.SortFunc is even more ergonomic with generics.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for custom sort.Interface implementations. Could sort.Slice or slices.SortFunc be used instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This implements sort.Interface with three methods just to sort by name. sort.Slice or slices.SortFunc would be much simpler.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use sort.Slice or slices.SortFunc. Implementing sort.Interface is only needed for complex cases.

items.text: Are there tests for new logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New functionality should have corresponding tests that verify its behavior. Tests are not optional in Go - the testing package is built into the language.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code is a liability. Tests catch regressions, document expected behavior, and give confidence when refactoring. Go makes testing easy - there is no excuse to skip it.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new functions and methods have corresponding test functions. Are edge cases and error paths covered?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new validateOrder function has no tests. Could you add tests for valid input, empty fields, and boundary conditions?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every new behavior deserves a test. Go makes testing a first-class concern.

items.text: Are test function names descriptive (`TestCreateUser_DuplicateEmail_ReturnsError`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test function names should describe what is being tested, the scenario, and the expected outcome: TestCreateUser_DuplicateEmail_ReturnsError.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails, the name is the first thing you see. A descriptive name tells you exactly what broke without reading the test body.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test function names. Do they follow a pattern like TestFunction_Scenario_ExpectedResult? Can you understand what they test from the name alone?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test TestProcess does not describe what scenario it covers. Consider TestProcess_EmptyInput_ReturnsError or similar.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name tests: TestUnit_Scenario_ExpectedBehavior. The name should explain the failure.

items.text: Are table-driven tests used for multiple input/output scenarios?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When testing multiple input/output scenarios, use table-driven tests. Define test cases as a slice of structs and loop over them with t.Run.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Table-driven tests reduce boilerplate, make it easy to add new cases, and produce clear output showing which case failed. They are the idiomatic Go testing pattern.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for repeated test patterns with different inputs. Could they be consolidated into a table-driven test?
baseContent.exampleComment: These five test functions all follow the same pattern with different inputs. Consider consolidating them into a table-driven test.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use table-driven tests for multiple scenarios. They are idiomatic, concise, and easy to extend.

items.text: Are test helpers using `t.Helper()` for clear error reporting?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test helper functions should call t.Helper() to mark themselves as helpers. This makes error output point to the calling test, not the helper function.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without t.Helper(), test failures show the line number in the helper function rather than the test that called it. This makes debugging harder.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test helper functions. Do they call t.Helper() as the first line? Are they accepting *testing.T as a parameter?
baseContent.exampleComment: The assertNoError helper does not call t.Helper(). When it fails, the error points to assertNoError instead of the test that called it.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Call t.Helper() in test helpers so errors point to the test, not the helper.

items.text: Are subtests used (`t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) {...})`) for organization?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T){...}) to create subtests. Subtests organize test output, can be run individually with -run, and enable parallel execution within a test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Subtests make test output readable, enable selective test execution, and work with table-driven tests. They also allow each subtest to run in parallel.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test functions. Are there multiple assertions that could be organized as subtests? Are table-driven tests using t.Run?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test function has multiple unrelated assertions. Using t.Run for each would make failures clearer and allow running them individually.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use t.Run for subtests. They organize output and can be run individually with -run.

items.text: Are tests parallel where possible (`t.Parallel()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests that do not share state can be parallelized with t.Parallel(). This speeds up test suites by running independent tests concurrently.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Serial test execution wastes time when tests are independent. Parallel tests also help detect race conditions that only appear under concurrency.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests modify shared state. If not, do they call t.Parallel()? Are table-driven subtests using t.Parallel()?
baseContent.exampleComment: These tests are independent and do not share state. Adding t.Parallel() would speed up the test suite.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Call t.Parallel() in tests that do not share state. It speeds up the suite and catches races.

items.text: Are test fixtures in `testdata/` directory?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test fixture files (sample inputs, golden files, configs) should be stored in a testdata/ directory. Go tooling ignores testdata/ in builds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The testdata/ directory is a Go convention. The go tool ignores it during builds, and go vet skips it. It keeps test data organized and out of production builds.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for test fixture files. Are they in testdata/? Are there fixture files scattered in the package root?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test JSON file is in the package root. Move it to testdata/ to follow Go convention.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Store test fixtures in testdata/. It is the standard Go convention.

items.text: Are golden files used for complex output comparisons?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Golden files store expected output for complex results. Tests compare current output against the golden file and update it with a flag (-update) when the output intentionally changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: For complex outputs (HTML, JSON, protobuf), inline expected values are unwieldy. Golden files keep tests readable and make intentional changes easy to review in diffs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for complex expected values hardcoded in tests. Would a golden file be clearer? Is there a -update flag mechanism?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test has a 50-line expected JSON string inline. Consider using a golden file in testdata/ for easier maintenance.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use golden files for complex expected output. Provide an -update flag to regenerate them.

items.text: Are tests testing behavior, not implementation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should verify observable behavior (inputs produce expected outputs) not internal implementation details (which functions are called, field order, etc.).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests coupled to implementation break when code is refactored, even if behavior is unchanged. Behavior tests give confidence to refactor safely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests verify outputs and side effects, or if they check internal details like function call counts or private field values.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test verifies that processOrder calls validateOrder internally. If we refactor the internals, the test breaks even though behavior is correct. Test the output instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test what the code does, not how it does it. Behavior tests survive refactoring.

items.text: Are assertions clear and specific (use `testify` or `go-cmp` for readable diffs)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test assertions should produce clear, descriptive failure messages. Use testify or go-cmp for readable diffs, especially for complex structs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A failure message like 'not equal' does not help. 'got user.Name="Bob", want "Alice"' tells you exactly what went wrong.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test assertions. Do failure messages include the actual and expected values? Are complex struct comparisons using go-cmp or testify?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test just checks if err != nil without including the error in the message. Use t.Fatalf("unexpected error: %v", err) so you can see what went wrong.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Include actual and expected values in failure messages. Use go-cmp or testify for complex comparisons.

items.text: Are tests independent (no reliance on execution order or global state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should be self-contained and not depend on execution order or shared global state. Tests should set up their own preconditions and clean up after themselves.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Dependent tests are fragile - they pass in one order but fail in another. They also make it impossible to run individual tests in isolation or in parallel.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests modify global state, share variables, or assume a specific execution order. Can each test pass when run alone?
baseContent.exampleComment: TestUpdate assumes TestCreate ran first and left data in the database. Each test should set up its own data so they can run independently.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Each test should create its own data and not depend on execution order or shared state.

items.text: Is `t.Cleanup()` used for test resource cleanup?

baseContent.whatItMeans: t.Cleanup() registers a function to run after the test completes. Use it for cleaning up resources like temporary files, database records, or server instances.
baseContent.whyItMatters: t.Cleanup() is called even if the test panics, unlike defer which only runs when the function returns. It keeps setup and teardown close together and works with subtests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test setup code. Are resources cleaned up? Is t.Cleanup() used for teardown? Are there resource leaks in tests?
baseContent.exampleComment: The temporary database created in this test is never cleaned up. Use t.Cleanup to drop it after the test.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use t.Cleanup() for test teardown. It runs even on panic and works with subtests.

items.text: Are timeouts or deadlines set for tests that could hang?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests that involve networking, channels, or goroutines should have timeouts or deadlines to prevent hanging indefinitely.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A hanging test blocks the entire CI pipeline. Timeouts ensure tests fail fast with a clear error instead of timing out at the CI level with no useful message.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check tests involving network calls, channel operations, or goroutines. Do they have timeouts? Is context.WithTimeout used?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test waits on a channel with no timeout. If the goroutine never sends, the test hangs forever. Add a select with a timeout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add timeouts to tests that wait on channels, goroutines, or network calls. Never hang CI.

items.text: Are mock/fake/stub interfaces used, not concrete types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test doubles should be based on interfaces, not concrete types. Define small interfaces for dependencies and create mock/fake implementations for testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Testing against concrete types forces you to instantiate real dependencies (databases, HTTP servers, etc.). Interfaces allow lightweight fakes that make tests fast and isolated.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the code under test depends on interfaces or concrete types. Are test doubles implementing the same interface?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test creates a real database connection. Define a UserStore interface and use a fake implementation for faster, isolated tests.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Depend on interfaces, test with fakes. It makes tests fast, isolated, and reliable.

items.text: Are integration tests tagged with build tags or in separate packages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Integration tests that require external services (databases, APIs) should be separated from unit tests using build tags or separate packages so they can be run selectively.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Running integration tests locally requires infrastructure. Separating them lets developers run fast unit tests locally and integration tests in CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests that connect to real services. Are they tagged with build tags (//go:build integration) or in _test packages?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test requires a running PostgreSQL instance but is not tagged as an integration test. Add //go:build integration so it only runs when explicitly requested.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tag integration tests with build tags. Let developers run unit tests fast without infrastructure.

items.text: Are benchmarks added for performance-critical code (`func BenchmarkXxx(b *testing.B)`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Performance-critical code paths should have benchmarks (func BenchmarkXxx(b *testing.B)) to measure and track performance over time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without benchmarks, performance regressions go undetected until they hit production. Benchmarks provide a baseline and make it easy to compare before/after.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new performance-sensitive code has benchmarks. Are hot paths covered? Can benchmarks be run to detect regressions?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new serialization function is called per request. Could you add a benchmark so we can track its performance?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Benchmark performance-critical code. It is the only way to detect regressions early.

items.text: Are benchmark results stable and meaningful (use `b.ResetTimer()`, `b.ReportAllocs()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Benchmarks should use b.ResetTimer() to exclude setup time, b.ReportAllocs() to track allocations, and produce stable results by avoiding external dependencies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Benchmarks that include setup time or depend on network calls produce noisy, misleading results. Stable benchmarks enable meaningful before/after comparisons.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check benchmark implementations. Is b.ResetTimer() called after setup? Is b.ReportAllocs() used? Are results stable across runs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This benchmark includes the database connection setup in the timing. Call b.ResetTimer() after setup so only the actual operation is measured.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use b.ResetTimer() after setup and b.ReportAllocs() for meaningful benchmarks.

items.text: Are fuzz tests added for parsers and validators (`func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F)`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Fuzz tests (Go 1.18+) generate random inputs to find edge cases in parsers, validators, and deserializers. They are specified with func FuzzXxx(f *testing.F).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fuzz tests find crashes, panics, and undefined behavior that humans would never think to test. They are especially valuable for code that handles untrusted input.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new parsers, validators, or input handlers. Do they have fuzz tests? Are seed values provided with f.Add()?
baseContent.exampleComment: This URL parser handles untrusted input. A fuzz test would help find edge cases that cause panics or incorrect parsing.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fuzz-test parsers and validators. They find bugs humans would never think to write tests for.

items.text: Are tests run with `-race` in CI?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The Go race detector should be enabled in CI with go test -race. It detects data races - concurrent access to shared memory without proper synchronization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data races cause undefined behavior in Go. The race detector is the most effective tool for catching them. It should be part of every CI pipeline.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration files. Is -race passed to go test? Is it enabled for all packages?
baseContent.exampleComment: Our CI runs go test ./... without -race. Please add -race to catch data races.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
# CI script
go test ./...

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI script
go test -race -count=1 -timeout=10m ./...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always run go test -race in CI. Data races are undefined behavior.

items.text: Are data races fixed, not worked around?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data races detected by the race detector should be properly fixed with synchronization (mutexes, channels, atomics), not suppressed or worked around.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Data races are undefined behavior. Working around them (e.g., adding sleeps, accepting intermittent failures) leaves a ticking time bomb that can corrupt data or crash the service.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if race detector findings are addressed with proper synchronization. Are there workarounds like sleep or retry loops instead of real fixes?
baseContent.exampleComment: Adding a sleep here masks the race condition but does not fix it. The counter needs atomic operations or a mutex to be accessed safely from multiple goroutines.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Fix data races with proper synchronization. Sleeps and retries are not fixes.

items.text: Are handlers using `http.HandlerFunc` or implementing `http.Handler`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP handlers should use the standard http.HandlerFunc signature or implement http.Handler. This makes them composable with middleware and standard library tools.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The standard handler signature enables composition with any middleware, router, or HTTP tool in the Go ecosystem. Custom signatures require adapters and reduce interoperability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP handler signatures. Do they match func(http.ResponseWriter, *http.Request) or implement http.Handler?
baseContent.exampleComment: This handler has a custom signature that includes extra parameters. Consider using closures or dependency injection to match the standard http.HandlerFunc signature.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the standard http.HandlerFunc signature. Inject dependencies via closures or struct methods.

items.text: Are middleware functions composable and focused?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Middleware should do one thing (logging, auth, CORS, etc.) and be composable. Each middleware should wrap an http.Handler and return an http.Handler.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Single-purpose middleware is reusable across endpoints. Composable middleware can be stacked in any order and tested independently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check middleware implementations. Does each do one thing? Do they accept and return http.Handler? Can they be composed?
baseContent.exampleComment: This middleware handles both authentication and logging. Consider splitting into two separate middleware so they can be applied independently.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concern per middleware. Compose them together in the handler chain.

items.text: Is request validation done early (before business logic)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Validate and parse the incoming request (headers, body, query params) at the beginning of the handler, before any business logic executes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Early validation gives clear error messages to clients, prevents wasted work, and keeps business logic clean. It also reduces the attack surface.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check handler functions. Is validation done before calling service methods? Are error responses returned early for invalid input?
baseContent.exampleComment: The handler calls the service before validating the request body. If the body is malformed, we waste a database query. Validate first.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate input first, then process. Fail fast with clear error messages.

items.text: Are response types consistent (JSON structure, error shapes)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API responses should follow a consistent JSON structure for both success and error cases. Error responses should have a predictable shape.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent response formats make client code harder to write and maintain. A standard error shape lets clients handle errors uniformly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check response formatting. Do all endpoints use the same error response structure? Is the JSON shape documented?
baseContent.exampleComment: Some endpoints return errors as {"error": "..."} and others as {"message": "...", "code": 400}. Let us standardize on one format.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Standardize response formats. Consistent error shapes make clients easier to write.

items.text: Are HTTP status codes correct and specific?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP responses should use the correct, most specific status code. Use 201 for created, 404 for not found, 409 for conflict, etc. - not 200 for everything.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Correct status codes enable proper client-side error handling, caching, and monitoring. Using 200 for errors breaks HTTP semantics and confuses clients.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP response status codes. Are they specific and correct for each scenario? Is 500 used for server errors and 4xx for client errors?
baseContent.exampleComment: This returns 200 OK with an error message in the body. Use 404 Not Found so clients and monitoring tools can handle it correctly.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use correct, specific HTTP status codes. They are part of your API contract.

items.text: Are request timeouts set via `http.Server.ReadTimeout`, `WriteTimeout`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP servers should configure ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout to prevent slow clients from tying up resources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without timeouts, a slow or malicious client can hold a connection open indefinitely, consuming server resources and enabling slowloris-style denial of service attacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check http.Server configuration. Are ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout set? Are they appropriate for the use case?
baseContent.exampleComment: The HTTP server has no timeout configuration. A slow client could hold connections open indefinitely. Please set ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
server := &http.Server{
    Addr:         ":8080",
    Handler:      mux,
    ReadTimeout:  5 * time.Second,
    WriteTimeout: 10 * time.Second,
    IdleTimeout:  120 * time.Second,
}
server.ListenAndServe()

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout on HTTP servers. No timeouts means no protection.

items.text: Are graceful shutdown patterns implemented (`server.Shutdown(ctx)`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP servers should implement graceful shutdown using server.Shutdown(ctx), which stops accepting new connections and waits for existing requests to complete.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without graceful shutdown, in-flight requests are killed during deployments, causing errors for users. Graceful shutdown lets active requests finish before the server exits.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check server startup code. Is there a signal handler? Does it call server.Shutdown with a timeout context?
baseContent.exampleComment: The server uses ListenAndServe without graceful shutdown. In-flight requests will be killed during deployments. Add signal handling and Shutdown.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Implement graceful shutdown. Listen for signals, call Shutdown, and let in-flight requests finish.

items.text: Are protobuf best practices followed (see Java+Protobuf checklist for proto schema review)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC services using Protocol Buffers should follow protobuf best practices for schema design: field numbering, backwards compatibility, and proper use of oneof, repeated, and map fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Protobuf schemas are API contracts. Poor schema design leads to painful migrations, broken compatibility, and confusing APIs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check .proto files for proper field numbering, reserved fields for removed items, and use of wrapper types for nullable fields.
baseContent.exampleComment: This proto message removes field number 3 without reserving it. Future use of field number 3 will cause deserialization bugs. Add 'reserved 3;'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow protobuf best practices. Schema changes are permanent API decisions.

items.text: Are interceptors used for cross-cutting concerns (logging, auth, metrics)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC interceptors (middleware) should handle cross-cutting concerns like logging, authentication, metrics, and tracing. Do not duplicate this logic in every handler.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without interceptors, every gRPC handler must implement its own logging, auth, and metrics. This leads to inconsistency and forgotten checks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check gRPC handlers. Do they contain logging, auth, or metrics code that should be in interceptors? Are interceptors registered on the server?
baseContent.exampleComment: Every gRPC handler manually checks auth tokens. This should be a unary interceptor so it cannot be forgotten for new endpoints.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use gRPC interceptors for auth, logging, metrics, and tracing. Do not repeat in every handler.

items.text: Are streaming RPCs used appropriately (not for simple request/response)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC streaming RPCs (server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional) should be used for real streaming use cases, not for simple request/response patterns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Streaming adds complexity to both server and client code. For simple request/response, unary RPCs are simpler, easier to test, and have better tooling support.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check streaming RPC definitions. Is streaming justified? Could a unary RPC with repeated fields or pagination serve the same purpose?
baseContent.exampleComment: This server-streaming RPC returns at most 10 items. A unary RPC with a repeated field would be simpler and easier to use.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use streaming RPCs for real-time data, large results, or push notifications. Use unary for simple request/response.

items.text: Are deadlines propagated through context?

baseContent.whatItMeans: gRPC deadlines should be set by the client and propagated through context to all downstream calls. The server should respect context cancellation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without deadline propagation, a timed-out client request continues executing on the server, wasting resources. Context propagation ensures all downstream work stops when the deadline passes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check gRPC client calls. Are deadlines set? Does the server pass the context to downstream calls?
baseContent.exampleComment: This gRPC client call has no deadline. If the server is slow, the client will block indefinitely. Add a deadline with context.WithTimeout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// No deadline - blocks forever if server is slow
resp, err := client.GetUser(context.Background(), &pb.GetUserRequest{Id: "123"})

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set deadlines on gRPC client calls and propagate context to downstream services.

items.text: Do functions accept interfaces and return structs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions should accept interfaces (to be flexible) and return concrete types (to be useful). This gives callers maximum flexibility while providing full functionality.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Accepting interfaces allows callers to pass any implementation. Returning structs gives callers access to all methods and fields, not just what an interface exposes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check function signatures. Do parameters use interfaces where appropriate? Do return types use concrete structs?
baseContent.exampleComment: This function accepts a concrete *PostgresDB but only calls Query and Exec on it. Accept a DB interface instead so callers can use any database.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Accept interfaces, return structs. It maximizes flexibility for callers.

items.text: Are functional options used for configurable constructors (`WithTimeout(d time.Duration)`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use the functional options pattern for constructors with many optional parameters. Define option functions like WithTimeout(d time.Duration) that modify a config struct.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Functional options provide a clean, extensible API that does not break when new options are added. They are self-documenting and provide sensible defaults.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check constructors with many parameters. Would functional options make the API cleaner? Are there config structs with many optional fields?
baseContent.exampleComment: This constructor has 8 parameters, most of which are optional. Consider the functional options pattern for a cleaner API.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use functional options for constructors with optional parameters. They are clean, extensible, and self-documenting.

items.text: Are exported APIs minimal and intentional?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A library's exported API surface should be minimal. Only export types, functions, and methods that consumers need. Internal details should be unexported.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every exported name is a commitment. Once consumers depend on it, you cannot change it without breaking them. A small API is easier to learn, document, and maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check exported names. Is each one necessary for consumers? Could any be unexported without breaking the public API?
baseContent.exampleComment: The package exports 15 types and 30 functions. Most seem to be internal helpers. Could we reduce the exported surface to just the 5 types and 10 functions consumers actually need?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Export only what consumers need. Every exported name is a permanent commitment.

items.text: Are breaking changes avoided or clearly documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Public API changes should be backwards-compatible. If breaking changes are unavoidable, they should be clearly documented and use a major version bump.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Breaking changes force all consumers to update simultaneously. In a shared library, this can cascade across many teams and services.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check changes to exported names, signatures, and behavior. Are they backwards-compatible? Is semantic versioning followed?
baseContent.exampleComment: Renaming this exported function breaks all existing callers. Could we add the new name as an alias and deprecate the old one instead?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid breaking changes. Add new functions and deprecate old ones instead of renaming.

items.text: Are allocations minimized in hot paths (reuse buffers, avoid unnecessary copies)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In performance-critical code paths, minimize heap allocations by reusing buffers, using sync.Pool, avoiding unnecessary pointer indirection, and pre-allocating slices.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each heap allocation creates work for the garbage collector. In hot paths handling thousands of requests per second, excessive allocations increase GC pause times and latency.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run benchmarks with -benchmem. Check for allocations in hot paths. Are buffers reused? Are slices pre-allocated? Are there unnecessary pointer conversions?
baseContent.exampleComment: This function allocates a new buffer on every call and it runs per-request. Consider using sync.Pool to reuse buffers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Minimize allocations in hot paths. Use sync.Pool, pre-allocate, and reuse buffers.

items.text: Are string/byte conversions minimized (`[]byte(s)` allocates)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Converting between string and []byte ([]byte(s) or string(b)) allocates new memory because Go strings are immutable. Minimize these conversions in hot paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each string/byte conversion copies the entire data. In hot paths processing large strings, this wastes memory and CPU time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for string(b) and []byte(s) conversions in hot paths. Can the code work with one type consistently?
baseContent.exampleComment: This converts []byte to string and back to []byte in the same function. Could we work with []byte throughout to avoid the copies?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid unnecessary string/byte conversions. Use bytes package functions to work with []byte directly.

items.text: Is `sync.Pool` used for frequently allocated, short-lived objects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: sync.Pool reduces GC pressure by reusing frequently allocated, short-lived objects. Common candidates include buffers, encoders/decoders, and temporary structs in hot paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In high-throughput services, allocating and freeing thousands of buffers per second creates GC pressure. sync.Pool amortizes the allocation cost.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check hot paths for repeated allocations of the same type. Would sync.Pool reduce GC pressure? Are pooled objects properly reset before reuse?
baseContent.exampleComment: This allocates a new bytes.Buffer on every request. With 10K req/s, that is 10K allocations per second. sync.Pool would significantly reduce GC pressure.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use sync.Pool for high-frequency allocations. Always Reset objects before reuse.

items.text: Are database queries efficient with proper indexes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database queries should be efficient with proper WHERE clause indexes. Avoid N+1 queries, full table scans, and unbounded result sets.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inefficient queries are the most common performance bottleneck in Go services. A missing index can turn a 1ms query into a 10-second table scan.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new queries. Are WHERE clause columns indexed? Are there N+1 query patterns (querying in a loop)? Are result sets bounded with LIMIT?
baseContent.exampleComment: This queries users by email but there is no index on the email column. Under load, this will become a performance bottleneck.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// Single JOIN query
rows, err := db.QueryContext(ctx, `
    SELECT o.id, o.user_id, u.name
    FROM orders o
    JOIN users u ON u.id = o.user_id
    WHERE o.status = $1
    LIMIT 100
`, "pending")

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use JOINs instead of N+1 queries. Ensure indexes exist for WHERE clause columns.

items.text: Are HTTP response bodies fully read and closed (prevent connection reuse issues)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP response bodies must be fully read (drained) and closed to allow the underlying TCP connection to be reused by the connection pool.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If the body is closed without being fully read, the connection cannot be reused and must be closed. This forces new TCP handshakes for every request, dramatically reducing throughput.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP client code. Is the body fully read before closing? Use io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body) to drain unneeded bodies.
baseContent.exampleComment: The response body is closed but not fully read on the error path. This prevents connection reuse. Drain it with io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body).

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Drain and close response bodies. Undrained bodies prevent connection reuse.

items.text: Are large payloads streamed, not buffered entirely in memory?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Large data transfers should be streamed using io.Reader/io.Writer rather than loading the entire payload into memory with io.ReadAll.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Buffering a 1GB file upload into memory requires 1GB of RAM per concurrent request. Streaming processes data in small chunks with constant memory usage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for io.ReadAll on potentially large inputs. Could the data be streamed with io.Copy or processed incrementally?
baseContent.exampleComment: This reads the entire uploaded file into memory. For large files, this could exhaust memory. Stream it directly to disk or the destination.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Stream large payloads. Use io.Copy instead of io.ReadAll for data you can process incrementally.

items.text: Is `pprof` profiling data considered for performance-sensitive changes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For performance-sensitive changes, consider using Go's built-in pprof profiler to measure CPU usage, memory allocations, and goroutine counts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Guessing at performance problems leads to premature optimization. pprof shows exactly where time and memory are spent, guiding targeted optimizations.
baseContent.howToVerify: For performance-critical changes, has profiling been done? Are pprof endpoints exposed in the service? Are benchmarks showing the improvement?
baseContent.exampleComment: This change claims to improve performance but there is no profiling data. Could you run pprof before and after to quantify the improvement?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Profile before optimizing. pprof shows where time and memory are actually spent.

items.text: Are connection pools tuned (`sql.DB.SetMaxOpenConns`, `SetMaxIdleConns`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Database connection pools (sql.DB) should have MaxOpenConns, MaxIdleConns, and ConnMaxLifetime configured based on the expected load and database capacity.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The default sql.DB has unlimited open connections. Under load, this can overwhelm the database. Too few idle connections cause latency from connection creation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check sql.DB configuration. Are SetMaxOpenConns, SetMaxIdleConns, and SetConnMaxLifetime called? Are the values appropriate for the load?
baseContent.exampleComment: The database connection pool has no limits configured. Under load, it could open thousands of connections and overwhelm PostgreSQL. Set reasonable limits.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
db, err := sql.Open("postgres", connStr)
// No pool configuration - defaults are unlimited

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Configure connection pool limits. Unlimited connections will overwhelm your database under load.

items.text: Is batch processing used for bulk operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Bulk operations (inserting 1000 rows, sending 1000 notifications) should be batched rather than processed one at a time.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Individual operations have per-call overhead (network round trips, transaction overhead). Batching amortizes this overhead across many items.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for loops that perform individual operations. Could they be batched? Are there N individual INSERT statements that could be a single bulk INSERT?
baseContent.exampleComment: This inserts records one at a time in a loop. Batching into groups of 100 with a single INSERT would be much faster.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Batch bulk operations. One round trip with 100 items beats 100 individual round trips.

items.text: Is user input validated and sanitized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All user input (HTTP parameters, headers, request bodies, file uploads) must be validated and sanitized before use. Never trust client-supplied data.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input is the root cause of most security vulnerabilities: injection, XSS, path traversal, buffer overflows, and denial of service.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check every point where user input enters the system. Is it validated for type, length, format, and range? Is it sanitized before use in queries, templates, or commands?
baseContent.exampleComment: The user-supplied 'name' parameter is used directly in the response without sanitization. This could enable XSS. Validate and escape it.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all input: type, length, format, range. Sanitize before use in output contexts.

items.text: Are SQL queries parameterized (using `$1`, `?` placeholders, not `fmt.Sprintf`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: SQL queries must use parameterized placeholders ($1, ?) for user input, never string concatenation or fmt.Sprintf. This prevents SQL injection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection is one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities. It allows attackers to read, modify, or delete database data and potentially execute system commands.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all SQL queries. Are user inputs passed as parameters? Is fmt.Sprintf or string concatenation used to build queries?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: This query uses fmt.Sprintf with user input, enabling SQL injection. Use parameterized queries with $1 placeholders.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// Parameterized query - safe from injection
rows, err := db.QueryContext(ctx, "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = $1", email)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: ALWAYS use parameterized queries. Never build SQL with string concatenation.

items.text: Are secrets not hardcoded or logged?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens, certificates) must not be hardcoded in source code or written to logs. Use environment variables, secret managers, or config files.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded secrets end up in version control where anyone with repo access can find them. Logged secrets are visible in monitoring systems and log aggregators.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hardcoded strings that look like credentials. Check log statements for sensitive data. Are secrets loaded from environment variables or secret managers?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: This API key is hardcoded in the source file. Move it to an environment variable or secret manager.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never hardcode secrets. Never log secrets. Use environment variables or secret managers.

items.text: Are TLS configurations secure (min TLS 1.2, no weak ciphers)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: TLS configurations should enforce TLS 1.2 as a minimum, disable weak cipher suites, and use proper certificate validation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Weak TLS configurations allow man-in-the-middle attacks, eavesdropping, and protocol downgrade attacks. TLS 1.0/1.1 have known vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check tls.Config settings. Is MinVersion set to tls.VersionTLS12 or higher? Is InsecureSkipVerify set to false?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: InsecureSkipVerify is set to true, which disables certificate verification. This allows man-in-the-middle attacks.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enforce TLS 1.2+. Never set InsecureSkipVerify to true in production.

items.text: Are file paths sanitized to prevent directory traversal (`filepath.Clean`, `filepath.Rel`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: User-supplied file paths must be sanitized to prevent directory traversal attacks (../../etc/passwd). Use filepath.Clean and filepath.Rel to restrict access.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Directory traversal allows attackers to read or write arbitrary files on the server, potentially accessing secrets, configs, or system files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where user input is used in file paths. Is filepath.Clean used? Is the resulting path validated to be within the allowed directory?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: The user-supplied filename is used directly in os.Open without sanitization. An attacker could use ../../etc/passwd to read system files.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Sanitize file paths. Validate that resolved paths stay within the allowed directory.

items.text: Are crypto operations using `crypto/rand`, not `math/rand`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cryptographic operations (generating tokens, keys, nonces) must use crypto/rand, not math/rand. math/rand is predictable and not suitable for security.
baseContent.whyItMatters: math/rand uses a deterministic PRNG. An attacker who knows the seed can predict all future values, compromising tokens, session IDs, and cryptographic operations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for math/rand usage. Is it used for anything security-sensitive? Should it be crypto/rand instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: This generates session tokens with math/rand, which is predictable. Use crypto/rand for security-sensitive random values.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use crypto/rand for security-sensitive random values. math/rand is predictable.

items.text: Are dependencies scanned for vulnerabilities (`govulncheck`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dependencies should be scanned for known vulnerabilities using govulncheck or similar tools. Vulnerable dependencies are a common attack vector.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Third-party dependencies can contain known vulnerabilities. Running govulncheck in CI catches these before they reach production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CI configuration. Is govulncheck or a similar tool running? Are vulnerable dependencies updated?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: govulncheck reports a known vulnerability in this dependency version. Please update to the patched version.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI pipeline
go install golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest
govulncheck ./...

# Or in go.mod, keep dependencies updated:
go get -u ./...
go mod tidy

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run govulncheck in CI. Keep dependencies updated to avoid known vulnerabilities.

items.text: Are input size limits enforced to prevent denial of service?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All input from untrusted sources must have size limits to prevent denial of service. This includes request bodies, file uploads, headers, and query parameters.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without size limits, an attacker can send a 10GB request body and exhaust server memory, taking down the service for all users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check input handling. Are size limits set with http.MaxBytesReader, io.LimitReader, or header-based limits?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: This endpoint reads the request body with no size limit. A malicious client could exhaust server memory. Add http.MaxBytesReader.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enforce input size limits on all untrusted input. Use http.MaxBytesReader for HTTP bodies.

items.text: Are authentication and authorization checked for every endpoint?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every API endpoint must verify that the requester is authenticated (who they are) and authorized (what they can do). No endpoint should be accidentally unprotected.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single unprotected endpoint can expose sensitive data or allow unauthorized actions. Auth middleware ensures consistent enforcement across all endpoints.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new endpoints. Are they behind auth middleware? Are there endpoints that should require authentication but do not?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: The new /admin/users endpoint has no authentication check. It should be behind the auth middleware.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
mux.HandleFunc("/api/users", listUsers)         // protected
mux.HandleFunc("/api/admin/delete", deleteUser)  // oops - no auth!

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
// Auth middleware applied to all API routes
apiRouter := mux.NewRouter().PathPrefix("/api").Subrouter()
apiRouter.Use(authMiddleware)

apiRouter.HandleFunc("/users", listUsers)
apiRouter.HandleFunc("/admin/delete", requireRole("admin")(deleteUser))

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use auth middleware for all protected routes. Never rely on developers remembering to add auth per-handler.

items.text: Are HTTP clients not following redirects to untrusted hosts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: HTTP clients should not automatically follow redirects to arbitrary hosts. An attacker could redirect requests to internal services (SSRF) or credential-harvesting sites.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Server-side request forgery (SSRF) lets attackers access internal services through your server. Uncontrolled redirects can also leak authentication headers to third parties.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check HTTP client configurations. Is the redirect policy configured? Are redirects limited to trusted hosts?
baseContent.exampleComment: BLOCKER: The HTTP client follows redirects without restriction. An attacker could redirect to internal services. Set a CheckRedirect policy.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Control redirect behavior. Limit redirects to trusted hosts to prevent SSRF.

items.text: Is `go.mod` tidy (`go mod tidy` leaves no diff)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Running go mod tidy should produce no diff. It removes unused dependencies and adds missing ones. A dirty go.mod or go.sum means dependencies are not in sync.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An untidy go.mod may contain unused dependencies that bloat builds or missing dependencies that cause build failures in CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run go mod tidy and check for changes. Is the go.mod committed in a tidy state?
baseContent.exampleComment: go mod tidy shows changes. Please run it and commit the updated go.mod and go.sum.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI check
go mod tidy
git diff --exit-code go.mod go.sum

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always commit a tidy go.mod. Run go mod tidy before committing.

items.text: Are new dependencies justified and reviewed for quality and maintenance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New dependencies should be evaluated for necessity, quality, maintenance status, and license compatibility before being added.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each dependency is a supply chain risk. Unmaintained or low-quality dependencies can introduce vulnerabilities, bugs, and bloat.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new entries in go.mod. Are they necessary? Are they well-maintained? Could the functionality be implemented with the standard library?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a dependency for a single utility function. The same thing can be done in 10 lines with the standard library. Consider removing the dependency.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Justify every dependency. A small standard library solution is often better than a third-party package.

items.text: Are indirect dependencies understood (no unexpected transitive deps)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Be aware of what indirect dependencies (transitive dependencies) your direct dependencies pull in. They can introduce unexpected packages into your build.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An indirect dependency could introduce security vulnerabilities, license issues, or build size bloat that you did not expect.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review go.sum changes. Are there unexpected new indirect dependencies? Run go mod graph to understand the dependency tree.
baseContent.exampleComment: Adding this one library pulls in 15 indirect dependencies including a C binding. Are we OK with that complexity?

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# Check dependency tree
go mod graph | grep "new-dependency"

# Check for vulnerabilities in all deps
govulncheck ./...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Understand your transitive dependencies. They are part of your supply chain.

items.text: Is the Go version in `go.mod` appropriate for the project?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The Go version directive in go.mod should match the team's target Go version. Bumping it enables new language features but may break compatibility with older toolchains.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The go directive controls which language features are available. Bumping it inadvertently can break builds for developers or CI environments on older versions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the go directive in go.mod. Was it intentionally bumped? Does it match the CI and team environment?
baseContent.exampleComment: The go directive was bumped from 1.21 to 1.22. Was this intentional? All team members and CI need to be on Go 1.22+.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Match the go directive to your team's minimum supported Go version.

items.text: Are build tags used correctly for platform-specific code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Build tags (//go:build) are used to include or exclude files based on OS, architecture, or custom conditions. They should be correct and tested.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect build tags can cause code to be silently excluded from builds, leading to missing functionality or compilation errors on specific platforms.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check //go:build tags. Are they correct for the intended platforms? Are the tagged files tested in CI for their target platforms?
baseContent.exampleComment: This file has //go:build linux but contains code that should also run on macOS. Update the build tag to //go:build linux || darwin.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use //go:build (not +build) and test on all target platforms.

items.text: Is `go vet` clean?

baseContent.whatItMeans: go vet runs static analysis to find common bugs: printf format mismatches, unreachable code, suspicious constructs, and more. It should pass with no warnings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: go vet catches real bugs that the compiler misses. It is fast, built-in, and has a very low false-positive rate.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run go vet ./... on the changed packages. Are there any warnings? Are they addressed?
baseContent.exampleComment: go vet reports a printf format mismatch: %d for a string argument. Please fix the format specifier.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI pipeline
go vet ./...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run go vet in CI. It catches real bugs with almost no false positives.

items.text: Are linter warnings addressed (`golangci-lint`, `staticcheck`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Linter warnings from golangci-lint, staticcheck, or similar tools should be addressed. Linters catch code quality issues, potential bugs, and style violations.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Linters catch issues that are easy to miss in review: unused variables, shadowed errors, inefficient code, and security concerns.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run the project's linter configuration. Are there new warnings? Are existing warnings being suppressed without justification?
baseContent.exampleComment: golangci-lint reports an unused variable and a shadowed error variable. Please address these warnings.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# CI pipeline with golangci-lint
golangci-lint run ./...

# Common useful linters:
# - errcheck: unchecked errors
# - gosec: security issues
# - govet: suspicious constructs
# - staticcheck: bugs and simplifications
# - unused: unused code

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Run linters in CI. Address warnings rather than suppressing them.

items.text: Are `//go:generate` commands documented and reproducible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: //go:generate directives should be documented, and the generated output should be committed or reproducibly generatable in CI.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without documentation, future developers will not know how to regenerate the code. Without reproducibility, builds may differ across environments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check //go:generate directives. Are they documented in the README or Makefile? Can someone new to the project run them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This //go:generate directive depends on a tool that is not in go.mod. Please add it to tools.go or document the installation step.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document go:generate commands. Track generator tools in tools.go.

items.text: Is the binary size reasonable (no unnecessary dependencies linked)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The compiled binary should not be unexpectedly large due to unnecessary dependencies or debug information included in production builds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large binaries increase deployment time, container image size, and memory usage. Unnecessary dependencies contribute to binary bloat.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare binary size before and after changes. Are there new dependencies that significantly increase size? Are build flags stripping debug info for production?
baseContent.exampleComment: The binary size increased by 15MB after adding this dependency. Is there a lighter alternative?

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
# Production build with stripped debug info
CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -ldflags="-s -w" -o myapp ./cmd/myapp

# Check what is contributing to binary size
go build -o myapp ./cmd/myapp
go tool nm myapp | sort -n -k2 | tail -20

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Monitor binary size. Use -ldflags="-s -w" for production builds. Question large new dependencies.

items.text: Are structured logs used (`slog`, `zap`, or `zerolog`) instead of `log.Printf`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use structured logging (slog, zap, zerolog) instead of unstructured log.Printf. Structured logs produce key-value pairs that are easily searchable and parseable.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unstructured logs are hard to search, filter, and aggregate. Structured logs enable powerful queries in log aggregation tools like Elasticsearch, Datadog, or CloudWatch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for log.Printf or fmt.Printf used for logging. Should they use slog, zap, or zerolog instead?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses log.Printf for operational logging. Our team standard is slog. Structured fields make it much easier to search and filter.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: log.Printf("user %s created order %s for $%.2f", userID, orderID, amount)

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
slog.Info("order created",
    slog.String("user_id", userID),
    slog.String("order_id", orderID),
    slog.Float64("amount", amount),
)

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use structured logging. It makes logs searchable and machine-parseable.

items.text: Are log levels used appropriately (debug for verbose, info for operational, error for failures)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Log levels should be used correctly: Debug for development verbosity, Info for operational events, Warn for unusual but handled conditions, Error for failures requiring attention.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect log levels cause noise (too many errors) or blindness (important events logged at debug). Proper levels enable filtering and alerting.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check log statements. Are levels appropriate? Are errors logged at Error level? Are routine operations at Info? Are debug details at Debug?
baseContent.exampleComment: This logs 'user not found' at Error level, but it is an expected condition (the user may not exist). Use Info or Warn instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
// Appropriate levels
slog.Debug("cache lookup", slog.String("key", key))       // verbose tracing
slog.Info("order created", slog.String("order_id", id))   // operational event
slog.Warn("slow query", slog.Duration("duration", dur))   // unusual but handled
slog.Error("database error", slog.Any("error", err))      // failure needing attention

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use correct log levels. Error means something is wrong. Info means business as usual.

items.text: Are metrics exposed (Prometheus counters, gauges, histograms) for new features?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features should expose relevant metrics (request counts, latencies, error rates, queue depths) via Prometheus or similar metrics systems.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without metrics, you cannot monitor the health of your service, set up alerts, or diagnose performance issues. You cannot improve what you cannot measure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new endpoints or features have metrics. Are there counters for requests, histograms for latencies, and gauges for resource usage?
baseContent.exampleComment: The new payment endpoint has no metrics. Could you add a request counter and latency histogram so we can monitor it?

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Expose metrics for new features: request counts, latencies, and error rates at minimum.

items.text: Are traces propagated through context (OpenTelemetry)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Distributed traces should be propagated through context.Context using OpenTelemetry or similar tracing libraries so requests can be traced across service boundaries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without trace propagation, you cannot follow a request across microservices. Distributed tracing is essential for debugging latency and failures in distributed systems.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the service uses tracing. Are trace contexts propagated through function calls? Are spans created for significant operations?
baseContent.exampleComment: This service call does not propagate the trace context. Add OpenTelemetry span creation so we can trace requests end-to-end.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Propagate traces through context. Create spans for significant operations.

items.text: Are health check endpoints (`/healthz`, `/readyz`) updated?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Health check endpoints (/healthz for liveness, /readyz for readiness) should be updated when new dependencies are added that affect the service's ability to serve traffic.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Kubernetes and load balancers use these endpoints to route traffic and restart unhealthy instances. Incorrect health checks cause traffic to be sent to broken instances.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new dependencies (databases, external services) should be included in readiness checks. Are health endpoints returning accurate status?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new Redis dependency but the readiness check does not verify Redis connectivity. A broken Redis connection would cause silent failures.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update readiness checks when adding new dependencies. Health checks must reflect reality.

items.text: Are feature flags used for gradual rollout?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New features, especially risky ones, should be behind feature flags for gradual rollout and quick rollback without deploying code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Feature flags let you enable features for a percentage of users, quickly disable broken features, and decouple deployment from release.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new features have feature flags. Is there a mechanism for gradual rollout? Can the feature be disabled without a deploy?
baseContent.exampleComment: This new payment flow changes behavior for all users immediately. Consider putting it behind a feature flag for gradual rollout.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use feature flags for risky changes. They enable gradual rollout and instant rollback.

items.text: Are configuration values validated at startup (fail fast)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Configuration values should be validated when the application starts. If required config is missing or invalid, the app should fail immediately with a clear error.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Failing fast at startup catches configuration errors before the service starts serving traffic. Late failures (when the config is first used) are harder to diagnose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check configuration loading. Are required values validated? Does the app exit with a clear error if config is invalid?
baseContent.exampleComment: The database URL is read from the environment but not validated at startup. If it is empty, the service will start but fail on the first request. Validate at startup.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all configuration at startup. Fail fast with clear error messages.

items.text: Are graceful shutdown and drain patterns implemented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Services should implement graceful shutdown: stop accepting new connections, drain in-flight requests, close resources, and then exit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hard shutdowns kill in-flight requests, corrupt data, and leave connections in bad states. Graceful shutdown ensures clean transitions during deployments.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the main function. Is there signal handling? Does it stop accepting connections, wait for in-flight requests, and clean up resources?
baseContent.exampleComment: The service does not handle shutdown signals. During deployments, in-flight requests will be killed. Add graceful shutdown.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Implement graceful shutdown: stop, drain, clean up, exit. Handle SIGTERM and SIGINT.
