items.text: Are exported functions and types documented with godoc-style comments?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Exported functions, types, constants, and variables should have godoc-style comments. The comment starts with the name of the item: // FuncName does X.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Godoc comments are the primary documentation for Go packages. They appear in pkg.go.dev, IDE tooltips, and go doc output. Missing docs make packages harder to use.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check exported names in the diff. Do they have comments? Do comments start with the item name?
baseContent.exampleComment: The exported function ProcessBatch has no godoc comment. Please add one: // ProcessBatch processes a batch of items concurrently...

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Document all exported names. Start comments with the item name. It is the Go convention.

items.text: Are package-level comments present in a `doc.go` file for important packages?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Important packages should have a package-level comment, typically in a doc.go file, that explains the package's purpose, usage, and any important conventions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The package comment is the first thing developers see in godoc. It provides context that individual function docs cannot. doc.go keeps it separate from implementation code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check important packages. Do they have package-level comments? Are complex packages documented with usage examples?
baseContent.exampleComment: This package has no doc.go. A package comment explaining the retry policy conventions would help new developers.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Add package comments to important packages. Put them in doc.go for clarity.

items.text: Are complex algorithms or business rules explained in comments?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Complex algorithms, non-obvious business rules, and critical design decisions should be explained in comments near the code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Code shows what happens, not why. When the 'why' is not obvious, future developers will misunderstand, misuse, or incorrectly 'fix' the code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check complex logic in the diff. Would a developer new to the codebase understand why the code does what it does? Are edge cases explained?
baseContent.exampleComment: This pricing calculation has several non-obvious discount rules. A comment explaining the business logic would help future maintainers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Comment the why, not the what. Explain business rules, edge cases, and design decisions.

items.text: Are examples provided for non-obvious APIs (`func ExampleFoo() {...}`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Non-obvious APIs should have example functions (func ExampleFoo) that demonstrate usage. Examples appear in godoc and are compiled and run as tests.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Examples are the best documentation. They show real usage, are always correct (they compile and run), and appear prominently in pkg.go.dev.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new exported APIs. Would an example help consumers understand the usage? Are there ExampleXxx functions in the test files?
baseContent.exampleComment: The functional options pattern for NewClient is not obvious. An Example function would make the usage clear.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Write Example functions for non-obvious APIs. They are living, tested documentation.

items.text: Are breaking changes called out in the PR description?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Breaking changes (API changes, behavior changes, migration requirements) should be clearly called out in the PR description so reviewers and downstream consumers are aware.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hidden breaking changes cause production incidents when consumers upgrade. Explicit callouts ensure proper coordination, migration planning, and changelog entries.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for changes to exported APIs, behavior, or configuration. Are breaking changes listed in the PR description?
baseContent.exampleComment: This changes the return type of GetUser from *User to (*User, error). This is a breaking change for all callers. Please call it out in the PR description.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always call out breaking changes in the PR description. Surprises in production are never welcome.

items.text: Is the README updated for new setup steps, environment variables, or CLI flags?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a PR adds new setup steps, environment variables, CLI flags, or dependencies, the README should be updated so developers can set up the project correctly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An outdated README causes hours of wasted time for new developers. They try to follow the instructions, fail, and have to ask someone for help.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR adds new env vars, setup steps, or build requirements. Is the README updated to reflect them?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds a new REDIS_URL environment variable but the README does not mention it. Could you add it to the configuration section?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep the README in sync with reality. New setup steps, env vars, and flags must be documented.
